target.title: improve: ship 3D rendered lobster favicon for Control UI
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

The Control UI uses a flat, hand-drawn 2D lobster as its favicon and logo. This PR upgrades that mascot to a 3D-rendered version of the **same lobster**, giving the browser tab icon, the login screen logo, and the installed PWA icon a more polished, modern look without changing the brand identity.

## Why This Change Was Made

The four favicon assets in `ui/public/` are replaced in place, keeping the exact same filenames so no markup, manifest, or TypeScript changes are needed:

- `favicon.svg` — now a self-contained SVG that embeds the 3D bitmap as a base64 `data:` URI inside a single `<image>` element. Because the login page and agent logo already reference `favicon.svg`, they pick up the new art automatically.
- `favicon-32.png` — 32×32 raster of the 3D lobster.
- `apple-touch-icon.png` — 180×180 raster.
- `favicon.ico` — regenerated from the 3D art.

No external assets are introduced; `favicon.svg` remains a single, dependency-free file. `index.html`, `manifest.webmanifest`, and all referencing TS modules are untouched.

## User Impact

Users see the upgraded 3D lobster in their browser tab favicon, on the login screen, and as the installed PWA / Apple touch icon. No behavioral changes.

## Evidence

Before / after render of `favicon.svg` (2D vs 3D), both on a transparent background:

![favicon before and after](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/woohahahaaa/openclaw/pr-assets/pr-assets/favicon-before-after.png)

Local validation:

- `pnpm build` — passed (favicon assets correctly copied into `dist/control-ui/`).
- `pnpm check` — passed except for the pre-existing `npm-shrinkwrap.json is stale` guard on `main`, which is unrelated to this change (no dependency or `package.json` edits were made; `npm-shrinkwrap.json` is untouched).
- Targeted favicon tests passed: `src/ui/public-assets.test.ts`, `src/ui/views/agents-utils.test.ts`.

## AI assistance

This change is **AI-assisted**. The 3D lobster artwork was generated with an AI image tool, and the asset pipeline (resizing to favicon sizes, embedding the bitmap into a self-contained SVG, ico generation) was carried out by an AI agent. The contributor confirms understanding of the change: it only swaps four static favicon assets in `ui/public/` with no code-path changes.

target.title: fix(agents): expose sessions_spawn in embedded mode when allowAgents is configured
target.body:
## Summary

- In embedded mode (TUI, `--local`), the `sessions_spawn` tool was unconditionally excluded unless `allowGatewaySubagentBinding` was explicitly set.
- This made `agents.defaults.subagents.allowAgents` silently ineffective for TUI users — the tool was always missing even when valid target agents were configured.
- Fix: allow `sessions_spawn` to be exposed in embedded mode when `allowAgents` lists at least one target agent, matching the documented intent of the config field.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/agents/openclaw-tools.update-plan.test.ts` → 20 passed
- Production function verified via `node --import tsx` (see Real behavior proof below)

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix / [ ] Feature / [ ] Refactor / [ ] Docs / [ ] Security / [ ] Chore

## Scope

- [ ] Gateway / [ ] Skills / [ ] Auth / [ ] Memory / [ ] Integrations / [ ] API / [x] UI/UX / [ ] CI

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** `sessions_spawn` tool missing from TUI/embedded agent tool list even when `agents.defaults.subagents.allowAgents` is populated.

**Environment tested:** Node 24.x on Linux, `node --import tsx` production function call.

**Steps run after the patch:** Called `createOpenClawTools` from the patched source with a config that has `allowAgents: ['code-reader', 'test-agent']` but no `allowGatewaySubagentBinding`.

**Evidence after fix:**

```text
SUCCESS: sessions_spawn tool IS included when allowAgents is configured.
Tool name: sessions_spawn
```

**Observed result:** The `sessions_spawn` tool is correctly included in the tool inventory when `allowAgents` is configured, even in embedded mode.

**Not tested:** Live TUI session with actual sub-agent spawning through the tool. Config with empty `allowAgents` array (tool should still be excluded in that case).

Closes #91095

target.title: fix(memory): resolve embedding provider by authProviderId fallback
target.body:
## Summary

- When a user configures `memorySearch.provider` to `"google"`, the embedding provider lookup failed with "Unknown memory embedding provider: google" because the registered adapter id is `"gemini"` while the config-level provider name is `"google"`.
- The gemini memory embedding adapter (`extensions/google/memory-embedding-adapter.ts`) declares `authProviderId: "google"`.
- This fix adds an `authProviderId` fallback once in `getMemoryEmbeddingProvider`, the shared runtime resolver in `src/plugins/memory-embedding-provider-runtime.ts`, so all consumers (memory manager, Gateway `/v1/embeddings`, startup warnings, doctor checks) agree on the resolution.
- No duplicated call-site scans — the fallback lives in the single shared resolver.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/memory-core/src/memory/embeddings.test.ts` → 8 passed (includes regression test for "google" → "gemini")
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/gateway/embeddings-http.test.ts` → 36 passed
- Real production-path proof: registered the actual gemini adapter and verified resolution (see below)

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix / [ ] Feature / [x] Refactor / [ ] Docs / [ ] Security / [ ] Chore

## Scope

- [x] Gateway / [ ] Skills / [ ] Auth / [x] Memory / [ ] Integrations / [ ] API / [ ] UI/UX / [ ] CI

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** `memorySearch.provider: "google"` caused "Unknown memory embedding provider: google" in `--index` and `--deep` because the gemini adapter id ("gemini") did not match the config-level provider name ("google"). The fix adds a centralized `authProviderId` fallback so "google" resolves to the registered gemini adapter.

**Environment tested:** Node 22.x on Linux, `node --import tsx` calling the actual production `registerMemoryEmbeddingProvider` + `getMemoryEmbeddingProvider` from the fix branch with the real gemini adapter from `extensions/google/memory-embedding-adapter.ts`.

**Steps run after the patch:** Registered the production gemini adapter (mirroring what the Google plugin does at gateway startup), then called `getMemoryEmbeddingProvider("google")` — the exact code path that `--index` and `--deep` hit when `memorySearch.provider` is "google".

**Evidence after fix:**

```text
Registered gemini adapter:
  id: gemini
  authProviderId: google
  defaultModel: gemini-embedding-001

=== Reproducing #90786: memorySearch.provider = "google" ===

After fix: "google" resolves to adapter "gemini"
  -> This is the fix. Before, this returned undefined and threw:
     "Unknown memory embedding provider: google"

"gemini" direct lookup: OK

"nonexistent" lookup: undefined (correct)
```

**Observed result after the fix:** `getMemoryEmbeddingProvider("google")` correctly resolves to the gemini adapter via the centralized `authProviderId` fallback. Direct lookup by adapter id ("gemini") continues to work. Unknown providers return `undefined` with no false positives.

**Not tested:** Live Rocky Linux gateway with real Google AI Studio API key, full `openclaw memory status --index --agent main` run, and memory index rebuild.

Closes #90786

target.title: [codex] Integrate production dashboard stack
target.body:
## Summary

Integrates the production-grade dashboard / Codex-style Chat stack onto current `main`.

Included surfaces:
- Working Now surface
- Chat-native Projects
- Plan Mode cards
- Pursue Goal UI/state
- Approval cards
- Tool / proof / artifact cards
- Multi-agent work tree
- Truth / completion diagnostics in Chat and Dashboard
- Apple-style polish and accessibility
- Network / remote-proof approval cards

## What Problem This Solves

The dashboard stack was previously built and proven on scoped feature branches, but current `main` did not contain the integrated production dashboard experience. Users could not test the full Codex-style Chat/Dashboard surface from mainline without switching to old feature branches.

This PR ports the proven dashboard stack onto current `main`, resolves integration regressions, and keeps Control Director truth, Judge-completion, liveness, approval, and completion enforcement intact.

## Evidence

Local proof on branch `codex/dashboard-production-grade-mainline-20260619` at `0167f7ef76315813ee13875b5b49ffcba7feb928`:

- `pnpm test ui/src/ui/views/chat.test.ts ui/src/ui/app-chat.test.ts ui/src/ui/chat/grouped-render.test.ts ui/src/ui/chat/tool-cards.test.ts ui/src/ui/chat/control-director-diagnostics.test.ts`
- `pnpm tsgo:test:ui`
- `pnpm ui:smoke:chat-work-surface`
- `pnpm ui:smoke:chat-projects`
- `pnpm ui:smoke:chat-plan-mode`
- `pnpm ui:smoke:chat-pursue-goal`
- `pnpm ui:smoke:chat-approval-cards`
- `pnpm ui:smoke:chat-tool-proof-artifact-cards`
- `pnpm ui:smoke:chat-truth-diagnostics`
- `pnpm ui:smoke:chat-polish-a11y`
- `pnpm ui:smoke:chat-network-remote-approval-cards`
- `pnpm check:changed`

Remote proof:

- Workflow Sanity dashboard stack proof: https://github.com/SnowBelt/openclaw/actions/runs/27859008647
- Full non-Android CI: https://github.com/SnowBelt/openclaw/actions/runs/27859046907

Live dashboard proof:

- Verified the current-branch foreground Gateway served the integrated Control UI and the key dashboard/chat surfaces loaded successfully.

Additional shrinkwrap gate investigation:

- Local sandbox-only shrinkwrap timeout was traced to sandboxed npm registry/log-path restrictions, not branch code.
- `pnpm deps:shrinkwrap:check` passed for all 36 package directories outside the sandbox.
- Full `pnpm check:changed` passed outside the sandbox on the final SHA.

## Notes

- This is a draft PR for review. Do not merge without explicit maintainer approval.
- Android-inclusive CI is not included here; non-Android full CI passed.

target.title: fix #94919: [Bug]: Z.AI Coding-Plan: ECONNRESET triggers model fallback — fallback notice is invisible to the user in async contexts (cron jobs, sub-agents, isolated runs)
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: OpenClaw generated `↪️ Model Fallback: ...` notices when a child/async run switched from the selected model to a fallback model, but async users could still miss the notice because ACP parent relays ignored fallback lifecycle events and cron/isolated output normalization dropped leading fallback notice payloads when preferring final assistant text.
- Solution: Surface the existing fallback lifecycle notice at the async parent/delivery boundaries instead of changing model selection or provider transport.
- What changed: ACP parent stream relay now forwards `fallback` and `fallback_cleared` lifecycle events to the parent session; cron/isolated payload normalization preserves leading `isFallbackNotice` payloads when it collapses text output to `finalAssistantVisibleText`.
- What did NOT change: No provider fallback decision logic, retry/backoff behavior, Z.AI endpoint handling, undici dispatcher defaults, public provider config, schema, or new `fetchOptions` contract.
- Why it matters / User impact: A user who starts a cron job, isolated run, or ACP/sub-agent run now receives the model-swap notice in the same async-visible stream/result instead of discovering the fallback only from a style/voice change.
- Fixes #94919

## What Problem This Solves

When fallback from the selected model to a configured runtime fallback already happened, the fallback notice was authored by the runner but could be lost at async delivery boundaries. The fix keeps the source-of-truth fallback decision in the runner and projects the existing notice metadata through the two boundaries that were dropping it: ACP child lifecycle events to parent stream, and cron/isolated final-output normalization.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Existing model fallback notices now cross the two async boundaries that were losing them: ACP child lifecycle events to parent stream, and cron/isolated `finalAssistantVisibleText` output selection.
- **Real environment tested:** Windows Git Bash task worktree, Node v22.17.0 (repo warns it wants >=22.19.0), pnpm/vitest against the OpenClaw async relay and cron payload code paths. Live Z.AI credentials/global endpoint load were not available in this environment. This patch starts after OpenClaw has already decided and emitted a fallback notice; it does not change provider transport or credential behavior.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
  ```sh
  pnpm exec tsx <local ACP fallback proof harness outside the repo>
  ```
  The proof harness imports the actual OpenClaw modules from this PR checkout (`startAcpSpawnParentStreamRelay`, `emitAgentEvent`, `drainSystemEventEntries`), starts a real ACP parent relay for `agent:main:main-proof`, emits a child `lifecycle:fallback` event using the same `emitAgentEvent` stream contract used by `agent-runner.ts`, then drains the parent system-event queue.

  ```sh
  node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/isolated-agent.helpers.test.ts src/agents/acp-spawn-parent-stream.test.ts
  ```
  ```sh
  node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.runreplyagent.e2e.test.ts
  ```
  ```sh
  pnpm exec vitest run --reporter=verbose --config test/vitest/vitest.cron.config.ts src/cron/isolated-agent.helpers.test.ts
  ```
  ```sh
  pnpm exec vitest run --reporter=verbose --config test/vitest/vitest.agents.config.ts src/agents/acp-spawn-parent-stream.test.ts -t "relays model fallback lifecycle notices to the parent session"
  ```
  ```sh
  pnpm exec oxlint src/cron/isolated-agent/helpers.ts src/cron/isolated-agent.helpers.test.ts src/agents/acp-spawn-parent-stream.ts src/agents/acp-spawn-parent-stream.test.ts
  ```
  ```sh
  git diff --check
  ```
- **Evidence after fix:** The real module proof produced these parent-visible system events:
  ```json
  [
    {
      "text": "Started codex session agent:codex:acp:child-proof. Streaming progress updates to parent session.",
      "contextKey": "acp-spawn:proof-run-fallback:start"
    },
    {
      "text": "codex: ↪️ Model Fallback: minimax/MiniMax-M3 (selected zai/glm-5-turbo; timeout)",
      "contextKey": "acp-spawn:proof-run-fallback:fallback"
    }
  ]
  ```
  It ended with `REAL_BEHAVIOR_PROOF_OK fallback notice relayed to parent system events`, proving the fallback event reaches the parent session queue rather than remaining child-local.

  The runner already emits real lifecycle events for fallback transitions in `agent-runner.ts` with `stream: "lifecycle"`, `phase: "fallback"`, selected/active provider-model fields, and `reasonSummary`; this PR wires the ACP parent relay to that existing event shape rather than inventing a new downstream string scrape.

  The cron helper suite also passed with `Test Files 1 passed (1)` and `Tests 29 passed (29)`, including the new case that preserves a leading fallback notice payload before the final answer. The focused ACP relay regression passed with `Test Files 1 passed (1)` and `Tests 1 passed | 33 skipped (34)`. The two requested `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs ...` commands completed with exit code 0 in this checkout. Focused oxlint passed on the four changed files, and `git diff --check` passed. SonarLint was not run because `sonarlint` is not available on PATH in this environment (`command not found`).
- **Observed result after fix:** The fallback notice is retained as a deliverable cron/isolated payload before final assistant text, and an actual ACP child fallback lifecycle event is converted into a parent-visible system event with the same provider/model reference formatting as existing fallback notices.
- **What was not tested:** No live Z.AI/global endpoint load test, no real credentialed provider outage run, no new provider transport config, and no public `fetchOptions` contract. Those are outside this propagation-only patch and would be needed for a separate connection policy/config change.
- **Disclosure:** Live/external Z.AI gateway proof was unavailable in this contributor environment, so the after-fix proof is a local ACP module run against this PR checkout plus focused cron/ACP regressions and requested runner test commands.

## Review findings addressed

- **RF-001 / RF-002 / RF-006 / RF-012:** ClawSweeper requested redacted real output proof from a cron, isolated, ACP, or sub-agent run. Addressed by the real module proof above: it imports and executes the actual relay/event/system-event modules from this PR checkout, emits the same lifecycle fallback event shape that runner code uses, drains the parent session queue, and asserts the user-visible fallback notice is present.
- **RF-003 / RF-008:** The ACP relay is wired to the real fallback event shape emitted by `agent-runner.ts`: `stream: "lifecycle"`, `phase: "fallback"`, `selectedProvider`, `selectedModel`, `activeProvider`, `activeModel`, and `reasonSummary`. The proof uses `emitAgentEvent` and the parent system-event queue to show that shape is now visible to the parent session.
- **RF-004 / RF-007:** Maintainer policy confirmation is still acknowledged. This PR intentionally surfaces an already-generated internal status notice at async boundaries; if maintainers decide fallback notices should not be shown on some external surfaces, the narrow delivery points here are where that policy can be gated.
- **RF-005:** Guarded provider transport policy remains intentionally out of scope. This PR does not add or change provider connection options, dispatcher behavior, schemas, public config, or fallback selection logic.
- **RF-009 / RF-010 / RF-011:** Ran the requested `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs ...` commands and `git diff --check`; all completed successfully in the PR checkout. Also reran the focused cron/ACP regressions with verbose Vitest output for maintainer-readable pass summaries.

## Regression Test Plan

- **Target test file:** `src/cron/isolated-agent.helpers.test.ts` covers the cron/isolated final-text collapse path, and `src/agents/acp-spawn-parent-stream.test.ts` covers ACP child-to-parent stream relay.
- **Scenario locked in:** A leading `isFallbackNotice` payload remains in `deliveryPayloads` when `finalAssistantVisibleText` replaces ordinary text payloads; a child `lifecycle:fallback` event with selected `zai/glm-5-turbo`, active `minimax/MiniMax-M3`, and reason `timeout` emits the formatted fallback notice to the parent session.
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:** The regressions exercise the exact async boundary functions that dropped visibility, without needing flaky external provider failures or live credentials. They fail if future code again treats fallback notices as child-local text during cron output selection or ACP parent stream relay.

## Merge risk

- **Risk labels considered:** message-delivery, session-state, compatibility, security-boundary.
- **Risk explanation:** Message-delivery/session-state risk is real because this intentionally adds parent-visible status events and preserves fallback notice payloads in async delivery results. Compatibility risk is limited to surfacing a notice OpenClaw already generates. Security-boundary risk was reviewed because the issue also suggests provider transport config, but this PR does not add config/schema/dispatcher behavior or change SSRF/pinned dispatcher code.
- **Why acceptable:** The diff is focused to async projection/delivery boundaries and adds no new provider API surface. It only moves an already-authored internal status notice to the user-visible async surface, with tests and real module proof showing the notice is preserved and formatted. Related open PR scan found public-config-related PRs, but this patch deliberately does not implement the public provider `fetchOptions` contract and does not overlap those config/schema changes.

## Root Cause

- **Root cause:** Fallback notice generation and fallback state tracking were correct in the runner, but the async output boundaries did not preserve that status. `startAcpSpawnParentStreamRelay()` ignored fallback lifecycle phases, and `resolveCronPayloadOutcome()` rebuilt text-only cron delivery from `finalAssistantVisibleText` without carrying leading `isFallbackNotice` payloads.
- **Why this is root-cause fix:** The broken invariant was not provider failover itself; it was that an already-generated model fallback notice must reach the same async-visible output path as the final result. This patch fixes that invariant at the two source async projection owners: ACP lifecycle-to-parent relay and cron/isolated payload outcome selection. It does not mask the symptom downstream with string scraping or provider-specific Z.AI handling.
- **Fix classification:** Root cause fix
- **Maintainer-ready confidence:** High for notice propagation; medium for the broader issue because the separate per-provider connection-policy suggestion remains intentionally out of scope.
- **Patch quality notes:** The added `fallback`/`timeout` strings are test fixtures and the expected user-visible status text, not retry/default/fallback masking. The early `undefined` returns only skip malformed lifecycle events that cannot be formatted safely. The cron helper preserves only leading `isFallbackNotice` payloads, avoiding a broader status-notice delivery change.
- **Architecture / source-of-truth check:** Source of truth for fallback decisions stays in `agent-runner.ts` / `fallback-state.ts`; ACP relay now projects the runner lifecycle event to the parent session, and cron payload outcome now preserves the existing payload metadata before final delivery. Public provider config/schema/protocol remains unchanged; the provider connection-policy/fetch-options contract suggested in the issue is explicitly out of scope for this PR.

target.title: ci: add plugin SDK impact gate
target.body:
## Summary

- Adds a trusted-base Plugin SDK impact gate for public SDK and contract-adjacent PR changes.
- Accepts `plugin-sdk:*` labels, PR body classification, and trusted exact-head ClawSweeper markers while preventing classifications from lowering deterministic severity.
- Requires merged `openclaw/rfcs` PRs for breaking and architecture classifications; reports when maintainer approval is required for review policy enforcement.
- Keeps the gate scoped to trusted base scripts and adds focused test routing for the new workflow and scripts.

## Linked context

Related: https://github.com/openclaw/clawsweeper/pull/268

Maintainer requested a Plugin SDK CI gate in anticipation of ClawSweeper applying the authoritative `plugin-sdk:*` label separately.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Plugin SDK interface changes now trigger a dedicated CI policy gate.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on the PR branch.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs test/scripts/plugin-sdk-impact-policy.test.ts test/scripts/plugin-sdk-impact-check.test.ts test/scripts/test-projects.test.ts test/scripts/ci-workflow-guards.test.ts`; `git diff --check origin/main...HEAD`; `autoreview --mode branch --base origin/main`.
- Evidence after fix: Focused Vitest run passed 25 policy tests and 171 tooling/workflow tests; diff checks passed.
- Observed result after fix: The gate classifies SDK-impacting paths, handles renamed current/previous paths, validates RFC requirements, and consumes labels/ClawSweeper exact-head markers.
- What was not tested: Live GitHub Actions execution on an actual PR event before opening this PR.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: Post-open autoreview still flagged workflow event-context concerns for fork review events and issue-comment check attachment. Those need follow-up before merge.

## Tests and validation

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs test/scripts/plugin-sdk-impact-policy.test.ts test/scripts/plugin-sdk-impact-check.test.ts test/scripts/test-projects.test.ts test/scripts/ci-workflow-guards.test.ts`
- `git diff --check origin/main...HEAD`
- `autoreview --mode branch --base origin/main` found remaining workflow-context issues after PR creation; see current review state.

Regression coverage was added for path detection, deterministic classification, label/body/ClawSweeper marker precedence, RFC extraction, GitHub paging, maintainer approval helper behavior, issue-comment PR lookup, and changed-test routing.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes, for PR merge policy only.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes, this adds a trusted-base GitHub Actions gate that uses GitHub API tokens for PR/RFC metadata only.

What is the highest-risk area?

GitHub event/secret behavior for review-triggered reruns and external PRs.

How is that risk mitigated?

The workflow checks out only trusted base code and never PR head code. Remaining workflow event-context concerns are called out below and should be resolved before merge.

## Current review state

What is the next action?

Resolve post-open autoreview findings before merge.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?

- Fork `pull_request_review` runs cannot mint the App token needed for maintainer-team verification.
- `issue_comment` reruns do not attach the required check to the PR head SHA.
- Live GitHub Actions proof from this PR run.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

Local autoreview findings for rename handling, metadata rename classification, approval fallback, comment marker lookup, issue-comment PR lookup, and review-trigger behavior were addressed before opening. A final post-open autoreview still found the workflow-context gaps listed above.

target.title: fix: minSecurity inverted security rank order (#91283)
target.body:
## Summary

Fixed the `minSecurity()` function which had the security rank order backwards, treating `full` as the most restrictive instead of the least restrictive.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: Session-level `security="full"` configuration was being incorrectly clamped to agent's `security="allowlist"` due to inverted security ranking in `minSecurity()`.

**Real environment tested**: OpenClaw repository local checkout with Node.js.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
1. Identified the bug in `src/infra/exec-approvals.ts:1531` where the security order was `{ deny: 0, allowlist: 1, full: 2 }`
2. Fixed the order to `{ full: 0, allowlist: 1, deny: 2 }` to correctly represent restriction levels
3. Updated test cases in `src/infra/exec-approvals-policy.test.ts` to assert the correct behavior
4. Added comprehensive test coverage for all 9 security level combinations

**Evidence after fix**:
```
minSecurity("full", "allowlist") === "full"     // session full overrides agent allowlist ✓
minSecurity("deny", "full") === "full"          // full is less restrictive than deny ✓
minSecurity("allowlist", "deny") === "allowlist" // allowlist is less restrictive than deny ✓
```

**Observed result after fix**: The security ranking now correctly represents:
- `full: 0` - least restrictive (allows all commands)
- `allowlist: 1` - medium restriction (allows only whitelisted commands)  
- `deny: 2` - most restrictive (denies all commands)

Session overrides with `security="full"` can now properly override agent defaults with `security="allowlist"`.

**What was not tested**: Live execution with actual OpenClaw session overrides (would require running the full OpenClaw application with specific config combinations).

## Changes

- `src/infra/exec-approvals.ts`: Fixed minSecurity function security order
- `src/infra/exec-approvals-policy.test.ts`: Updated test cases to reflect correct behavior

## Related

- Fixes #91283

target.title: fix: minSecurity order inverted — full should be least restrictive
target.body:
`minSecurity` was treating `"full"` as rank 2 (most restrictive) and `"deny"` as rank 0 (least restrictive), which is backwards.

When `resolveExecHostApprovalContext` computes:

```ts
const hostSecurity = minSecurity(params.security, approvals.agent.security);
```

a session-level `security="full"` override was being clamped to the agent's `"allowlist"` because `order["full"]` (2) > `order["allowlist"]` (1), so `minSecurity` would always return the more restrictive value.

Note that `execSecurityFloorRank` in the same file already uses the *correct* order (`full=0, allowlist=1, deny=2`). This aligns `minSecurity` with that.

**Impact:** Users with `exec.security="full"` in session overrides or `exec-approvals.json` would still get `allowlist` enforcement unless the agent config was also `"full"` — making per-session security overrides effectively broken.

**Fixes:** #58691, #59079
**Closes:** #91283

## Summary

Fixes #53958.

Operators looking at Control UI Overview often need to answer a basic troubleshooting question quickly: which config file is this gateway actually using?

The gateway snapshot already includes `configPath`, so this change simply surfaces it in the Overview snapshot card:
- show the active config path when the handshake includes it
- keep the rest of the Overview flow unchanged
- add the new label across the existing localized UI bundles

## Tests and validation

```bash
git diff --check
```

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed:
- the Overview page should show the active config file path when the gateway exposes it in the handshake snapshot

Environment tested:

Evidence after the fix:
- `renderOverview()` now reads `snapshot.configPath` and renders it directly under the snapshot stats
- the underlying gateway snapshot schema already includes `configPath`, so this is a presentation-only change

Observed result:

What was not tested:
- a live Control UI session against a running gateway

## Risk

Low. This is a display-only Control UI change on top of existing snapshot data. No protocol, config, or gateway behavior changed.

target.title: refactor(memory): drop redundant agent-id scoping from qmd collection names
target.body:
## Summary

- **What:** drop the redundant `-<agentId>` scoping from managed qmd collection names. Each agent has its **own isolated qmd index** (`agents/<agentId>/qmd/index.sqlite`), so collection names can't collide across agents — the agent-id suffix adds nothing but hardcodes the agent id into collection identifiers.
- **How:** remove `scopeCollectionBase` (and two now-dead path helpers, `isPathInsideRoot` / `canonicalizePathForContainment`); name default collections by their base (`memory-root`, `memory-dir`), the session collection `sessions`, and extra collections by their explicit name. Reverse the legacy migration to **scoped→unscoped** (`migrateLegacyScopedCollections` / `deriveLegacyScopedName`) so existing per-agent indexes migrate cleanly.
- **Why migration is cheap:** qmd collections are a **rebuildable derived index** (not user data), so the migration drops the stale scoped collection and lets the unscoped one be (re)created + reindexed — no import, no data loss (consistent with this repo's "prefer delete/drop/rebuild over import for cache").
- **Outcome:** stable, agent-agnostic collection names; net **−42 LOC** of production code for the de-scope itself (removes `scopeCollectionBase` + two now-dead path helpers in `backend-config.ts`).
- **Orphan cleanup:** the scoped→unscoped migration is **kind-gated**. Engine-owned collections (`memory`/`sessions`, whose base names are engine-generated and not user-suppliable) are removed even when their pattern has drifted from the current default; user-named (`custom`) collections keep the path+pattern guards and are **never** auto-deleted. Without this, a pattern-drifted legacy engine collection lingered as an orphan and was re-walked + re-embedded by every `qmd update` — a recurring reindex cost the migration otherwise shipped. (Trade-off, stated for transparency: for engine kinds this intentionally drops the prior "a user coincidentally named a collection like a legacy scoped name" guard — safe because those base names cannot be user-supplied; `custom` retains it.)
- **Disambiguated custom-name upgrade (ClawSweeper P1):** when two custom collections share a name, the shipped scoped names disambiguated as `<base>-<agentId>-<n>` (e.g. `notes-main`, `notes-main-2`), but the unscoped probe `deriveLegacyScopedName(<base>-<n>)` is `<base>-<n>-<agentId>` (`notes-2-main`) — so `migrateLegacyScopedCollections` missed the second orphan, and qmd then rejected the new unscoped collection on the same path+pattern, stranding it under the legacy name. `tryRebindConflictingCollection` now confirms the collection qmd names in the add-conflict via `qmd collection show` (the only command that surfaces the path, reusing `enrichLegacyCollectionPath`) and rebinds it when path **and** pattern match; a parsed name alone never removes a collection, so user-owned path guards are preserved.
- **Stacked on #91253 (per ClawSweeper):** the first commit is the path-aware qmd-rebind fix (#91253); the remaining commits are the de-scope, a docs + session-migration-test follow-up, and the kind-gated orphan cleanup. The rebind is **required** for the migration to be safe — without path-aware metadata, an upgraded index holding both a stale *unscoped* collection (old path) and a current *scoped* one could keep the stale unscoped target active; with the rebind folded in, the stale unscoped collection is rebound to the current path. Once #91253 lands, this rebases to drop that first commit.

## Linked context

Closes #91259

Depends on / folds in #91253 (path-aware qmd collection rebind) — the first commit of this PR.

Related: #91265 (an earlier PR toward the same proposal; this one folds in the path-aware rebind so existing scoped indexes migrate safely, adds an upgrade-case regression, and includes live before/after behavior proof).

Requested by a maintainer or owner? No — follows from the proposal in #91259.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

Behavior addressed: managed qmd collection names dropping the redundant `-<agentId>` suffix; existing per-agent indexes migrate scoped→unscoped; and orphaned engine-owned legacy collections are cleaned up.

Real environment tested: (A) a live, self-hosted OpenClaw gateway (macOS), backend `qmd` (`@tobilu/qmd` 2.5.2) running this build, against an agent whose index already held scoped collections; (B) a scratch per-agent index driven by the real `QmdMemoryManager` + the real `qmd` 2.5.2 binary for the orphan-cleanup case; (C) a scratch in-container→host rebind driven by the real `QmdMemoryManager` + real `qmd` 2.5.2 binary (the sandbox-mode transition).

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```
# (A) live gateway — de-scope migration on an agent with existing scoped collections:
openclaw gateway restart
openclaw memory index --force --agent <agent>
sqlite3 <agent-qmd-index>.sqlite "select name,path from store_collections order by name;"

# (B) scratch index — seed two legacy scoped orphans (drifted pattern), run the engine (cli mode):
qmd collection add <ws>/memory --name memory-dir-<agent> --mask "**/*.txt"   # engine-owned (memory)
qmd collection add <ws>/notes  --name notes-<agent>      --mask "**/*.txt"   # user-owned   (custom)
# then QmdMemoryManager.create({ mode: "cli" }) runs the migration; list collections before/after.

# (C) sandbox-mode transition — seed a collection at a stale in-container root, run the engine:
qmd collection add <root>/home/node/.openclaw/teams/x/workspace --name workspace --mask "**/*.md"
# then QmdMemoryManager.create({ mode: "cli" }) with workspace=<host-root>; `qmd collection show workspace` before/after.
```

Evidence after fix:
```
# (A) live gateway (identifiers redacted):
# BEFORE (scoped names):
memory-root-<agent>   <WS>
memory-dir-<agent>    <WS>/memory
sessions-<agent>      <agent-state>/qmd/sessions
team-library-<agent>  <WS>/library/promoted
team-library          <WS-old>/library/promoted    # orphan duplicate
# AFTER (reindex):
memory-root / memory-dir / sessions / team-library  # all unscoped
# scoped (-<agent>) collections remaining: 0

# (B) scratch index, real qmd 2.5.2 + real engine (both orphans had a DRIFTED pattern):
# BEFORE:
memory-dir-proofagent  Pattern: **/*.txt   # engine-owned (memory)
notes-proofagent       Pattern: **/*.txt   # user-owned   (custom)
# AFTER (engine ran, mode=cli):
notes-proofagent       Pattern: **/*.txt   # LEFT  — user data never auto-deleted
memory-root / memory-dir / notes           # unscoped, engine-created from scratch
# memory-dir-proofagent : 1 -> 0  (engine-owned, removed)
# notes-proofagent      : 1 -> 1  (user-owned, preserved)

# (C) sandbox-mode transition, real qmd 2.5.2 + real engine:
# BEFORE: workspace -> .../home/node/.openclaw/teams/x/workspace   (stale in-container root)
# AFTER (engine ran, mode=cli): workspace -> .../host/workspace    (rebound to host root)
```

Observed result after fix: (A) all managed collections migrated to unscoped names (including `sessions`); `0` scoped collections remained; the prior orphan duplicate (`team-library` + `team-library-<agent>`) collapsed to a single `team-library`. (B) the engine-owned drifted-pattern orphan was removed while the user-owned one was preserved, and fresh unscoped collections (`memory-root`/`memory-dir`/`notes`) were created. (C) a collection pinned to a stale in-container path was rebound to the configured host workspace root.

What was not tested: launching an actual Docker sandbox container — the bind-mount + container-launch plumbing is OpenClaw platform infrastructure, outside this PR's diff. The in-container→host path transition this PR's code performs is proven with the real binary in (C) above and by a unit test (container→host rebind transition, #91253).

Proof limitations or environment constraints: (A) is redacted terminal output from a private fleet (paths/agent/team identifiers removed); (B) uses generic scratch identifiers and is reproducible by any maintainer on `qmd 2.5.2`.

Before evidence: shown inline in (A) above (the pre-reindex scoped names + orphan duplicate).

## Tests and validation

Commands run (clean checkout off `openclaw/openclaw` `main`, re-verified on the current head after the disambiguation fix):
- `pnpm test extensions/memory-core/src/memory` → **444 passed (40 files)**
- `pnpm test packages/memory-host-sdk` → **38 passed (9 files)**
- `pnpm check:test-types` (tsgo) → **clean, 0 errors**
- `oxfmt --check` + `oxlint` on the touched files → clean.

Regression coverage:
- Default + custom collection-name tests now assert the unscoped form.
- The unscoped→scoped migration tests were replaced with scoped→unscoped ones.
- **Upgrade-case regression (addresses the flagged compatibility risk):** an index holding BOTH a stale *unscoped* collection (old path) AND a current *scoped* one at the name switch → asserts the stale unscoped collection is rebound to the **current** path (via the folded-in `collection show` path enrichment), not left active.
- **Kind-gated orphan cleanup:** a rewritten migration-guard test — a `memory`-kind legacy scoped collection with a drifted pattern is now **removed** (genuinely fails against the pre-fix guard), while a `custom`-kind one with a drifted pattern is **left** — plus a contract test asserting every managed collection carries a `kind` (the safety gate depends on it).
- **Disambiguated custom-name upgrade regression:** two custom collections both named `notes` at different roots, with legacy `notes-<agentId>` / `notes-<agentId>-2` seeded in a real-`qmd`-shaped mock (name-only `collection list`, path-bearing `collection show`) → asserts the disambiguated orphan (`notes-<agentId>-2`) is removed and both unscoped collections (`notes`, `notes-2`) bind to their correct roots. Red against the pre-fix conflict path (the orphan is never removed), green after.

Multi-agent note: safe by construction — each agent has its own isolated qmd index, so unscoped names cannot collide across agents.

## Risk checklist

User-visible behavior change? Internal only — qmd collection identifiers (a derived index) change; no user-facing API/config change.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? Migration only: existing per-agent indexes migrate scoped→unscoped on the next `ensureCollections()` (drop stale scoped + recreate unscoped + reindex). No config keys changed.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? No.

Highest-risk area: the scoped→unscoped migration on existing indexes, and the kind-gated removal of legacy scoped collections.

How is that risk mitigated: removal only ever targets the exact derived legacy name `<base>-<agentId>` for a **desired** collection. For `custom` collections the path+pattern guards remain, so a user-named collection is never auto-deleted. For `memory`/`sessions` the base names are engine-generated and cannot be user-supplied, so the derived legacy name is unambiguously engine-owned; if such a collection is still configured it is simply re-added at the same path/pattern (net-zero, a reindex). qmd collections are rebuildable, so any dropped-then-recreated collection reindexes from source with no user-visible data loss.

## Current review state

Next action: maintainer review.

What is still waiting: CI + review.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed: ClawSweeper review findings on #91274 (docs example, session-migration coverage, scoped-duplicate upgrade case, orphan cleanup, and the disambiguated custom-name migration P1 — fixed via the show-verified conflict rebind).

target.title: fix(agents): include requester identity in sessions_send context (Fixes #38570)
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #38570.

Include the requester agent's configured `identity.name` in the `sessions_send` agent-to-agent prompt contexts.

This updates the three A2A prompt builders used by the flow:

- initial `sessions_send` request context
- bounded ping-pong reply prompts
- final announce prompt

The requester name is resolved from the requester session key through the configured agent identity and injected as:

`Agent 1 (requester) name: <identity.name>.`

Non-goals:

- no change to agent routing or access policy
- no change to target-agent identity handling
- no change to delivery behavior or ping-pong limits

## Tests and validation

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/tools/sessions-send-helpers.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed:

- A2A prompt context should explicitly tell the receiving agent the requester's configured identity name instead of forcing it to infer from the session key

Local proof:

- exercised the production helper surface that renders the A2A prompt text
- added assertions for all three prompt shapes so the rendered context now includes `Agent 1 (requester) name: Stevo.`

Observed result:

- helper tests passed after plumbing `requesterName` through the `sessions_send` tool and A2A flow

What was not tested:

- a full live multi-agent exchange in a running OpenClaw session

Why this is still meaningful:

- the user-visible behavior change is entirely in the prompt text built by the `sessions_send` helper flow
- the changed tests exercise the same prompt builders used by the tool at runtime

## Risk

Low risk.

This only adds one extra prompt line when the requester agent has a configured `identity.name`. It does not change session selection, visibility checks, channel routing, or tool authorization.

target.title: perf: split subagent registry runtime imports
target.body:
## Summary

- Split the subagent registry lazy runtime imports across three narrower runtime entries: `context-engine-init.runtime`, `context-engine-registry.runtime`, and `runtime-plugins.runtime`.
- Keep the existing `subagent-registry.runtime` build entry for live-update compatibility, but stop new subagent-registry code from loading that broad runtime surface.
- Add source-side wrapper entries so source-mode tests and built `dist` artifacts resolve the same relative runtime specifiers.
- Lazy-load the legacy context-engine compaction delegate only when `LegacyContextEngine.compact()` is invoked.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.config.ts src/agents/subagent-registry.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/agent.test.ts src/agents/subagent-registry.runtime-imports.source.test.ts src/infra/tsdown-config.test.ts` -> 7 files / 441 tests passed.
- `node scripts/tsdown-build.mjs && node scripts/runtime-postbuild.mjs` -> exited 0 after final amend.
- `pnpm deadcode:dependencies` -> exited 0.
- `pnpm deadcode:unused-files` -> exited 0.
- `pnpm lint` -> exited 0.
- `git diff --check` -> exited 0.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: subagent registry callers no longer pay the single broad `subagent-registry.runtime` lazy import when they only need context-engine initialization, context-engine resolution, or runtime plugin loading.

Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout at PR head `cb8f21c18474daefa934f2cf4343f0ff324114be`, rebuilt `dist` output, OpenClaw CLI invoked with a fresh temporary `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: after `node scripts/tsdown-build.mjs && node scripts/runtime-postbuild.mjs`, ran a built-artifact probe from the repository root with `node --input-type=module`. The probe invoked `node openclaw.mjs --version` under a temp OpenClaw state directory, found the generated `dist/subagent-registry-*.js` chunk containing the split dynamic import specs, and imported each stable runtime entry from `dist`.

Evidence after fix:

```text
openclaw_cli=OpenClaw 2026.6.2 (cb8f21c)
OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/var/folders/mb/c0c6q53x1hx2khlf_y8qc9qw0000gn/T/openclaw-subagent-split-proof-NzazcQ
built_registry_chunk=dist/subagent-registry-C0Ir8DD3.js
registry_spec_present ./context-engine-init.runtime=true
registry_spec_present ./context-engine-registry.runtime=true
registry_spec_present ./runtime-plugins.runtime=true
registry_legacy_combined_spec_present=false
context-engine-init.runtime.js exports=ensureContextEnginesInitialized
context-engine-registry.runtime.js exports=resolveContextEngine
runtime-plugins.runtime.js exports=ensureRuntimePluginsLoaded
subagent-registry.runtime.js exports=ensureContextEnginesInitialized,ensureRuntimePluginsLoaded,resolveContextEngine
```

Additional emitted stable entry sizes after runtime postbuild:

```text
119 dist/context-engine-init.runtime.js
101 dist/context-engine-registry.runtime.js
 55 dist/runtime-plugins.runtime.js
318 dist/subagent-registry.runtime.js
593 total
```

Observed result after fix: the built registry chunk contains the three narrower lazy runtime specs, does not contain the legacy combined `subagent-registry.runtime` spec, and all three new stable runtime entries import successfully from the rebuilt package. The retained compatibility `subagent-registry.runtime.js` still exports the same three symbols for existing live-update consumers.

What was not tested: full test suite, live gateway update survivor flow, and live subagent E2E.

target.title: feat(whatsapp): support opt-in group-name config
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #91174.

- Adds WhatsApp group config matching by exact current group name, gated behind `channels.whatsapp.dangerouslyAllowGroupNameMatching` at root or account scope.
- Keeps stable group JIDs as the preferred and highest-precedence key for group allowlists, require-mention overrides, tool policy, reactions, and group system prompts.
- Registers the new break-glass flag in schema/types, generated channel config metadata, security dangerous-flag collection, and docs.

reviewMetrics: added 1 WhatsApp config flag (`dangerouslyAllowGroupNameMatching`) at root/account scope; this matters before merge because mutable group-name matching affects group authorization and must remain explicit, audited break-glass behavior rather than an implicit fallback.

Docs:
- https://docs.openclaw.ai/channels/whatsapp
- https://docs.openclaw.ai/channels/groups
- https://docs.openclaw.ai/gateway/security

## Verification

target.title: fix(tui): persist canonical provider in session modelProvider
target.body:
## Summary

After a CLI-routed agent run (any model with `agentRuntime.id` pinned to a CLI backend such as `claude-cli` or `google-antigravity-cli`), the TUI footer renders `claude-cli/claude-sonnet-4-6` instead of `anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6` — leaking the implementation-routing id into the user-facing status line.

Root cause: `setSessionRuntimeModel(next, { provider: providerUsed, ... })` in `src/agents/command/session-store.ts` was called with the runtime id as `provider`, persisting it as `sessionInfo.modelProvider`. The footer renderer (`src/tui/tui.ts:~1183`) just reads that value.

Fix: canonicalize at the single setter call site using the existing `resolveCliRuntimeCanonicalProvider` helper (`src/agents/cli-backends.ts:245-271`). Only the persisted display value changes; the raw `providerUsed` continues to drive runtime-dependent logic (`isCliProvider`, CLI session bookkeeping).

## Scope

- `src/agents/command/session-store.ts` — call canonicalization helper before `setSessionRuntimeModel` in the non-preserve branch.
- `src/agents/command/session-store.test.ts` — vi.mock `../cli-backends.js` with the lookup convention used for `isCliProvider`; add failing-first test.

## Deliberately Out Of Scope

- The `preserveRuntimeModel` branch (lines 160-179) re-uses an already-persisted `entry.modelProvider`. After this fix that value is canonical for any new run; legacy stored sessions remain unchanged. No migration is shipped — stale entries naturally update on the next non-preserve run.
- Other consumers of `sessionInfo.modelProvider` (e.g. UI controls) are unaffected because the field's contract becomes *more* aligned with what they already render.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** TUI footer shows runtime id (`claude-cli/...`) instead of canonical provider (`anthropic/...`) when a model is routed through a CLI backend.

**Real environment tested:** macOS, source repo `/Users/rob/Development/openclaw`, branch `feature/canonical-modelprovider`. User config has `anthropic/claude-*` pinned to `agentRuntime.id = "claude-cli"`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```
pnpm exec vitest run src/agents/command/session-store.test.ts
pnpm tsgo:core
```

**Evidence after fix:** Terminal output, real run:

```
Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  74 passed (74)
```

`pnpm tsgo:core` exit=0.

The new test `canonicalizes CLI runtime-id provider before persisting modelProvider` was first written failing (`Expected: "anthropic" Received: "claude-cli"`) and then made green with the helper-based fix — confirming both the root cause and the resolution.

**Observed result after fix:** `sessionInfo.modelProvider` now persists the canonical provider id. With the same TUI footer renderer (`${modelProvider}/${model}`), the status line will display `anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6` instead of `claude-cli/claude-sonnet-4-6`.

**What was not tested:**

- Live TUI footer render against this source build (requires installing the source dist; deferred to user smoke after merge or via `pnpm gateway:dev`).
- Migration of pre-existing session-store entries that carry the old runtime-id format (none performed; legacy values heal on next run).

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: fix(telegram): route outbound sends through configured default account (#61012)
target.body:
## Summary

With multiple Telegram bot accounts configured, outbound sends via `openclaw message send --channel telegram` (and proactive/subagent sends) ignored the configured default account and always routed through the implicit env/top-level `"default"` bot. This makes the outbound account resolver honor the configured default when no account is specified, so:

1. `message send --channel telegram` with **no** `--account` uses the configured default bot.
2. `--account <id>` routes to that bot.
3. Subagent/proactive sends (which omit `--account`) use the correct bot identity.

## Root cause

`extensions/telegram/src/accounts.ts` → `resolveTelegramAccount()` passed the raw (possibly `undefined`) `accountId` straight into the shared `resolveAccountWithDefaultFallback()` helper:

```ts
return resolveAccountWithDefaultFallback({
  accountId: params.accountId, // undefined when no --account
  ...
});
```

When `accountId` is omitted, the helper normalizes `undefined` → the literal `"default"` account id and resolves that **first**. If the implicit `"default"` account has any credential — e.g. a top-level `channels.telegram.botToken` or `TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN` left over from a single-bot setup — the helper returns it immediately and never consults `resolveDefaultTelegramAccountId()`. So `channels.telegram.defaultAccount` (and the default-agent binding) was silently ignored and every send went out under the `"default"` bot. The helper's `resolveDefaultAccountId` fallback only fires when the `"default"` account has *no* credential — precisely the case where the bug doesn't bite.

The outbound path confirms this is the deciding point: the durable send adapter (`outbound-adapter.ts`) calls `sendMessageTelegram(to, text, { cfg, accountId })` with **no** explicit token, so `resolveTelegramAccount()` alone decides the bot/token.

The sibling channel `extensions/nextcloud-talk/src/accounts.ts` already does this correctly — it pre-resolves `params.accountId ?? resolveDefaultNextcloudTalkAccountId(cfg)` before calling the same helper.

## The fix

Mirror the nextcloud-talk resolver: resolve the configured default account id up front when none is provided, then pass that into the helper.

```ts
const resolvedAccountId = params.accountId ?? resolveDefaultTelegramAccountId(params.cfg);
return resolveAccountWithDefaultFallback({
  accountId: resolvedAccountId,
  ...
});
```

`resolveDefaultTelegramAccountId()` already encapsulates the correct precedence (default-agent binding → `channels.telegram.defaultAccount` → first listed / implicit `"default"`), so single-bot, env-only, explicit `--account`, and inbound routing are unchanged; only the previously-broken multi-bot default selection changes.

Scope note: `inspectTelegramAccount()` uses the same helper for capability/status discovery (not send routing). Its omitted-`accountId` callers either branch to the all-accounts union (`channel-actions.ts`) or are diagnostic, so they don't affect which bot sends; left out of this bug-scoped change.

## Verification

Done in an isolated linked worktree on `fix/issue-61012-telegram-default-account` (based on latest `upstream/main`). **OpenClaw was not run locally** — no gateway/CLI/`pnpm dev`/`openclaw …`. Verified by code review + focused Vitest unit tests only.

Added a focused colocated test in `extensions/telegram/src/accounts.test.ts` covering default-account selection (no flag) and explicit-account selection, and confirmed it fails before the fix and passes after:

- **Before** (fix one-liner reverted): `routes omitted-account sends through the configured defaultAccount (#61012)` fails — `AssertionError: expected 'default' to be 'secondary'` (1 failed | 35 passed).
- **After**: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/telegram/src/accounts.test.ts` → **36 passed**.
- **Regression** (send/account/outbound path): `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs` over `send.test.ts action-runtime.test.ts outbound-adapter.test.ts bot-message-dispatch.test.ts channel.gateway.test.ts channel.message-adapter.test.ts send.proxy.test.ts` → **345 passed (7 files)**.

Not tested: live multi-bot Telegram delivery (no real bots/tokens exercised). No tokens or secrets are included in the code, tests, or this PR.

Fixes #61012

target.title: fix(gateway): preserve abort after dispatch rejection
target.body:
## Summary

What problem does this PR solve?

- Preserves the terminal `chat.abort` outcome when `chat.send` dispatch rejects after the abort already completed.
- Preserves the real dispatch `error` outcome when abort arrives only during awaited error persistence, instead of letting the late abort mask or cache the error.
- Adds regressions for both race orderings and keeps stale abort tombstones from leaking into later same-id retries.

Why does this matter now?

- `chat.send` now caches terminal abort results for abort races. The post-dispatch rejection path still had two bad windows: abort-first could be overwritten by a late dispatch error, and error-first could expose `aborted` while the handler was still awaiting fallback persistence.
- The branch has been refreshed onto `main` after a merge conflict in the Gateway test file; the final pre-publish check against the latest `main` found no same-surface drift and a clean merge-tree.

What is the intended outcome?

- Same-run retry after an abort-first race observes the terminal timeout/aborted payload, not a synthetic dispatch error.
- Same-run retry after an error-first race observes the error while the error dedupe is live, and a later retry can start normally instead of replaying a stale abort marker.

What is intentionally out of scope?

- Client-side terminal ACK handling.
- Live provider/model E2E behavior.
- The original pre-dispatch attachment-preparation abort race, which is already fixed on `main`.

What does success look like?

- The PR diff stays limited to `src/gateway/server-methods/chat.ts` and `src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat-b.test.ts`.
- Focused Gateway regression, typecheck, lint, diff hygiene, production-handler proof, and real Gateway/WebSocket RPC proof all pass on the refreshed branch head.

What should reviewers focus on?

- The `.catch` path snapshots whether abort had already won before any awaited fallback persistence.
- The error-winning path closes abortability, clears late abort state, and writes same-run error dedupe before awaiting fallback persistence.
- The idempotent active-run cleanup prevents duplicate cleanup while removing the late abort/retry window.

## Linked context

Which issue does this close?

Fixes #90999

Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related?

Related #84176 and #84306.

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

No.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed:

Post-dispatch `chat.send` rejection could produce contradictory terminal state around `chat.abort`: abort-first races could be overwritten by a dispatch error, and error-first races could briefly expose/cache `aborted` before the final dispatch `error`.

- Real environment tested:

Linux source checkout at local conflict-repair head `e16d6f5fcfeabe193b49883fe89061dead8efad1`, Node/Vitest through repo scripts. The merge commit has parents `74dbae230e9e5ecf80739ec7a0939130fe9ef670` and `7e12a3326dd2396ca39ceee079f4304966e92fb9`; exact main CI run `27505476408` for that base was green when proof was captured. Pre-publish fetch advanced `upstream/main` to `6134c00657a247d6f7accc9182d0b901c4f26b48` (3 commits, no Gateway/proof-surface overlap); merge-tree stayed clean and PR-style diff stayed two Gateway files. Current-base checks were not fully green at prep time, so post-push PR checks are the CI source of truth.

- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```shell
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat-b.test.ts
node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-test-pr91000-conflict2-e16.tsbuildinfo
node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-pr91000-conflict2-e16.tsbuildinfo
node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs src/gateway/server-methods/chat.ts src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat-b.test.ts
git diff --check upstream/main...HEAD -- src/gateway/server-methods/chat.ts src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat-b.test.ts
OPENCLAW_PROBE_ROOT=/tmp/openclaw-pr91000-conflict2-20260615T010132+0800.5Emd8e node --experimental-test-module-mocks --import tsx /tmp/pr91000-behavior-probe-dynamic.mts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts src/gateway/pr91000-websocket-proof-dynamic.test.ts
```

- Evidence after fix:

Terminal capture from this branch, copied live output:

```shell
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat-b.test.ts
Test Files 2 passed (2)
Tests 82 passed (82)

$ node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-test-pr91000-conflict2-e16.tsbuildinfo
passed

$ node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-pr91000-conflict2-e16.tsbuildinfo
passed

$ node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs src/gateway/server-methods/chat.ts src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat-b.test.ts
Found 0 warnings and 0 errors.

$ git diff --check upstream/main...HEAD -- src/gateway/server-methods/chat.ts src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat-b.test.ts
no output

$ OPENCLAW_PROBE_ROOT=/tmp/openclaw-pr91000-conflict2-20260615T010132+0800.5Emd8e node --experimental-test-module-mocks --import tsx /tmp/pr91000-behavior-probe-dynamic.mts
source: production Gateway chatHandlers with controlled dispatch boundary and temporary config/session store
abort-first dispatch rejection: abortResponse.aborted=true, terminalDedupe.status=timeout, chatBroadcastStates=[aborted], errorBroadcastCount=0
error-first late abort: abortResponse.aborted=false, retryDuringPending.status=error, lateAbortTombstonePresentAfterError=false, midWindowChatBroadcastStates=[], chatBroadcastStates=[error], retry after deleting error dedupe starts

$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts src/gateway/pr91000-websocket-proof-dynamic.test.ts
gateway-core: 2 tests passed
gateway-server: 2 tests passed
gateway-client: 2 tests passed
Test Files 3 passed (3)
Tests 6 passed (6)
```

Real Gateway + WebSocket RPC proof artifact from the last command:

```text
proofKind: real Gateway server + WebSocket RPC harness with controlled dispatch boundary
branchHead: e16d6f5fcfeabe193b49883fe89061dead8efad1
abort-first dispatch rejection over WebSocket:
  chat.send ACK status=started
  chat.abort response aborted=true, runIds=[ws-proof-abort-first]
  dispatch saw AbortSignal.aborted=true
  chat broadcast states=[aborted]
error-first late abort over WebSocket:
  chat.send ACK status=started
  late chat.abort response aborted=false, runIds=[]
  same-id chat.send retry during pending fallback returned cached status=error with UNAVAILABLE
  mid-window chat broadcast states=[]
  final chat broadcast states=[error]
```

- Observed result after fix:

Abort-first dispatch rejection preserves the terminal timeout/aborted result and emits only the aborted chat state. Error-first dispatch rejection makes the error terminal before awaited fallback persistence; late abort returns `aborted:false`, same-id retry during the pending fallback receives the cached error, no aborted chat state is emitted, and retry after deleting the error dedupe can start normally.

- What was not tested:

No browser UI, native client, or live provider/model E2E run was performed. The strongest transport proof uses a real Gateway server plus real WebSocket RPC client, while controlling only the dispatch boundary through the repo's normal Gateway test seam.

- Proof limitations or environment constraints:

The race is deterministic once dispatch rejection, fallback persistence, and abort timing are controlled. The production-handler probe and WebSocket proof both control that boundary so they can falsify the two terminal-state orderings without depending on a flaky live provider race.

- Before evidence (optional but encouraged):

```shell
Main at merge parent `7e12a3326dd2396ca39ceee079f4304966e92fb9` still reproduced both bad windows in the diagnostic production-handler probe before this branch's fix:
abort-first: chat.abort returned aborted=true, terminalDedupe.status=error, chatBroadcastStates=[aborted,error]
error-first: late chat.abort returned aborted=true, retryDuringPending.status=timeout, lateAbortTombstonePresentAfterError=true, chatBroadcastStates=[aborted,error]
```

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat-b.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-test-pr91000-conflict2-e16.tsbuildinfo`
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-pr91000-conflict2-e16.tsbuildinfo`
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs src/gateway/server-methods/chat.ts src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat-b.test.ts`
- `git diff --check upstream/main...HEAD -- src/gateway/server-methods/chat.ts src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat-b.test.ts`
- `OPENCLAW_PROBE_ROOT=/tmp/openclaw-pr91000-conflict2-20260615T010132+0800.5Emd8e node --experimental-test-module-mocks --import tsx /tmp/pr91000-behavior-probe-dynamic.mts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts src/gateway/pr91000-websocket-proof-dynamic.test.ts`

What regression coverage was added or updated?

- Added/updated coverage for abort-before-dispatch-reject preserving terminal abort state.
- Added/updated coverage for dispatch-reject-before-late-abort preserving terminal error state, rejecting late abort, serving cached error to same-id retry while fallback persistence is pending, and allowing a later retry after error dedupe cleanup.
- Refreshed the branch onto `main` at `7e12a3326dd2396ca39ceee079f4304966e92fb9`; resolution keeps `removeTempDir(sessionDir)` cleanup and adjacent timing tests. Pre-publish fetch at `6134c00657a247d6f7accc9182d0b901c4f26b48` had no same-surface drift and clean merge-tree.

What failed before this fix, if known?

- The original abort-first regression failed because `chat:<runId>` cached `status:"error"` instead of the expected timeout/aborted payload.
- A later error-first probe found `chat.abort` could still return `aborted:true` during pending fallback persistence, and broadcasts could include both `aborted` and `error` for the same run.
- Current `main` still reproduced both bad windows before the refreshed branch was tested.

If no test was added, why not?

N/A.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes. Terminal abort/error ordering is now stable for post-dispatch rejection races.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No.

What is the highest-risk area?

Abort/error ordering in the `chat.send` async cleanup path, especially while fallback transcript persistence is pending.

How is that risk mitigated?

Both orderings are covered: abort already visible at dispatch rejection, and dispatch error already terminal before a late abort arrives. The additional WebSocket proof exercises the real Gateway server and RPC transport shape while keeping the race timing deterministic.

## Current review state

What is the next action?

Review the refreshed conflict-repair head and the updated real Gateway/WebSocket RPC proof.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?

After the branch/body refresh, CI and maintainer/bot review are still pending.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

No new bot comment was addressed in this refresh. The refresh updates the exact base/head/proof evidence after conflict repair while preserving the existing proof scope.

target.title: fix(agents): guard CLI MCP resume reuse
target.body:
### Summary
- Invalidate stored CLI session bindings when they have a strict `mcpResumeHash` but the current run cannot provide one.
- Add regression coverage for the one-sided resume-hash case while keeping legacy `mcpConfigHash` fallback for bindings that never stored a resume hash.

### Root cause
`resolveCliSessionReuse` only compared `mcpResumeHash` when both the stored binding and current run had one. If a stored CLI session had a strict resume hash but a later run could not compute the current resume hash, the helper fell back to the broader `mcpConfigHash` comparison and could reuse a session without proving the resumed MCP topology still matched.

### Real behavior proof
Behavior or issue addressed: CLI sessions created with a strict bundled-MCP resume hash should not be reused by a later run that cannot prove the same resumed MCP tool topology. The patch prevents a stored `mcpResumeHash` from silently downgrading to the less precise raw MCP config hash when the current run lacks `mcpResumeHash`.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout at `/Users/stellarfish/.codex/worktrees/5db8/openclaw-next-agent`, branch `codex/agent-runtime-followup`, macOS shell, Node `v22.22.2`, corepack pnpm `11.2.2`. target.title: feat(google): add Antigravity CLI backend
target.body:
## Summary

Adds a Google Antigravity CLI runtime backend that delegates OAuth and execution to the local `agy` binary while keeping the user-facing model identity on the existing Google provider.

Fixes #84527.

## Scope

Plugin surface:

- `extensions/google/cli-backend.ts` adds `buildGoogleAntigravityCliBackend()`.
- `extensions/google/index.ts` registers the backend in the bundled Google plugin.
- `extensions/google/setup-api.ts` registers the backend in setup hooks.
- `extensions/google/openclaw.plugin.json` advertises the backend, aliases, and Google-family metadata.
- `src/status/agent-runtime-label.ts` adds the display label `Antigravity CLI`.

Minimal core adds:

- `src/config/zod-schema.core.ts` allows `google-antigravity-cli` as a provider overlay id.
- `src/plugins/config-state.ts` maps the CLI backend id to the Google plugin.
- `src/config/model-input.ts` treats `google-antigravity-cli` as a Google-family provider for model ref normalization.

## Deliberately Out Of Scope

These are known follow-up issues for separate upstream PRs:

- Status display should resolve runtime labels via the same CLI runtime resolver used by execution.
- `session_status` should handle runtime refs explicitly or provide a clearer runtime override path.
- Compaction should not bind blindly to the current session provider/model; user config can pin a working compaction model meanwhile.
- TUI sticky error state after Gateway restart should reset on connection restoration.
- `current` vs explicit session store key behavior should be documented or made consistent in session status tooling.

## Phase 12 Follow-Up

This PR intentionally does **not** add a SQLite tool-call reader for `agy`. `agy --print` currently returns text, and any SQLite reader would be heuristic and tied to internal Antigravity storage formats. Tool-call rendering should be handled as a separate Phase 12 design/PR after this runtime path is stable.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** OpenClaw could not delegate Google chat completions to a local `agy` (Antigravity CLI) install. This PR wires the `google-antigravity-cli` provider end-to-end so a chat call routes through the CLI's already-authenticated OAuth session and returns a real Gemini response.

**Real environment tested:** macOS Darwin 25.5.0. Globally installed `openclaw` (dist v2026.6.1) patched with the PR's plugin + core changes. `agy` 1.0.6 with valid `~/.gemini/oauth_creds.json` (auto-refreshed during run after 16h expiry). User config `~/.openclaw/openclaw.json` enabling `google-antigravity-cli` with `gemini-3.5-flash`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```
openclaw agent \
  --model google-antigravity-cli/gemini-3.5-flash \
  --message "Reply exactly OK"
```

**Evidence after fix:** Live runtime `executionTrace` captured from the actual CLI invocation against the real Google OAuth backend (terminal output from a real OpenClaw setup, not a mock and not a unit-test stub):

```
duration: 13s (cli turn 4498ms)
executionTrace:
  winnerProvider: google-antigravity-cli
  winnerModel:    gemini-3.5-flash
  runner:         cli
  result:         success
  finalAssistantVisibleText: OK
```

Routing chain verified end-to-end: OpenClaw embedded -> `buildGoogleAntigravityCliBackend` factory -> `agy --print` -> `~/.gemini/oauth_creds.json` (auto-refreshed) -> Google Gemini API -> response `OK`.

Direct `agy` sanity outside OpenClaw (terminal output):

```
$ agy --model gemini-3.5-flash --print "Reply exactly OK"
OK
```

**Observed result after fix:** OpenClaw returns the live Gemini response (`OK`) via the new CLI runtime. `winnerProvider`, `winnerModel`, and `runner` all match the requested route. OAuth refresh completed inside the same invocation without manual re-auth.

**What was not tested:**

- Tool-call rendering through the CLI runtime (deferred to Phase 12; `agy --print` currently does not emit machine-parseable tool events).
- Vertex / API-key fallbacks for the same provider id (out of scope; this PR is OAuth-via-`agy` only).
- Windows / Linux paths (verified macOS only).

target.title: i18n(fr): expand French glossary with page titles and common terms
target.body:
## What

Expands `docs/.i18n/glossary.fr.json` from 19 to 52 entries.

The French glossary previously held only proper nouns kept in English (OpenClaw, CLI, Gateway, etc.), so the translation pipeline had no guidance for how to render descriptive page titles or common terms in French. This adds mappings so French output stays consistent across the localized docs.

## Changes

- **Page titles**: Getting Started, Quick Start, Model Providers, Provider directory, Channel Routing, Install overview, SDK overview, Setup Wizard Reference, CLI Setup Reference, macOS platform notes, Linux server, VPS hosting, Code mode, Creating skills, Web tools, Secure file operations, Agent runtimes, Talk mode, Steering queue, Capability Cookbook.
- **Common UI/technical terms**: troubleshooting → Dépannage, Settings → Paramètres, Credentials → Identifiants, Deployment → Déploiement, Overview → Vue d'ensemble, Status → État, Logging → Journalisation, Models → Modèles, Platforms → Plateformes.
- **Channel → Canal** (and Channels → Canaux) as the standard French rendering, since "channel" appears throughout the docs.

Product and protocol nouns (Skills, Plugin, Gateway, Webhook, ACP, TUI, …) are left in English, keeping the existing convention. All entries are plain `{source, target}` pairs — the shape the glossary loader supports — matching the existing 19 entries.

Native French speaker; happy to extend coverage further.

target.title: fix(acp): strip chat options for Claude ACP sessions
target.body:
## Summary
- preserve startup ACP runtime options without replaying them as post-start controls
- strip chat-only model/thinking options from managed Claude ACP startup and resume paths
- keep timeout/cwd runtime options intact and add regressions for spawn, initialize, and restart/resume paths

## Tests
- node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/agents/acp-spawn.test.ts src/acp/control-plane/manager.initialize-session.test.ts src/acp/control-plane/manager.runtime-handles.test.ts
- pnpm tsgo:core:all

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** Managed Claude ACP sessions should not fail because OpenClaw forwards chat/runtime controls such as model or thinking/effort as post-start Claude ACP config options. The patch strips those chat-only options for Claude ACP while preserving timeout/cwd handling.

- **Real environment tested:** Mac Mini M4 arm64 running the live OpenClaw gateway with managed Claude ACP enabled, plus the PR head source checkout at `cc542548a08a42eb950a393f7f7223c3af0aafe0` for focused regression tests.

- **Exact steps or command run after the patch:**
  1. Ran a live managed Claude ACP smoke through OpenClaw with deliberately bad chat-style options: `runtime=acp`, `agentId=claude`, `model=gpt-5.5`, `thinking=off`, task `Reply exactly ACP_SMOKE_OK and stop`.
  2. Read the resulting OpenClaw session history to confirm the Claude ACP session produced a normal assistant response instead of an ACP config-option failure.
  3. Ran the PR-head regression command: `node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/agents/acp-spawn.test.ts src/acp/control-plane/manager.initialize-session.test.ts src/acp/control-plane/manager.runtime-handles.test.ts`.

- **After-fix evidence:**
  Live OpenClaw/Claude ACP smoke output from the real gateway after the runtime fix:

  ```text
  openclaw sessions_spawn runtime=acp agentId=claude model=gpt-5.5 thinking=off task="Reply exactly ACP_SMOKE_OK and stop"
  status: accepted

  openclaw sessions_history <spawned Claude ACP session>
  user: Smoke test only. Reply exactly ACP_SMOKE_OK and stop. Do not inspect files or run commands.
  assistant: ACP_SMOKE_OK
  ```

  Redacted pre-fix live gateway failures from the same setup showed the rejected option this patch prevents:

  ```text
  2026-06-12T12:24Z AcpRuntimeError: Invalid value for config option effort: off: code=ACP_TURN_FAILED run=0b4a2d6a...
  2026-06-13T08:36Z AcpRuntimeError: Invalid value for config option effort: off: code=ACP_TURN_FAILED run=39f87d89...
  2026-06-13T09:46Z AcpRuntimeError: Invalid value for config option effort: off: code=ACP_TURN_FAILED run=01728a7d...
  2026-06-13T11:01Z AcpRuntimeError: Invalid value for config option effort: off: code=ACP_TURN_FAILED run=963d5346...
  ```

- **Observed result after fix:** The live managed Claude ACP smoke returned `ACP_SMOKE_OK`; it did not fail with the previous `Invalid value for config option effort: off` / `ACP_TURN_FAILED` error. On PR head `cc542548a08a42eb950a393f7f7223c3af0aafe0`, the focused regression command passed 86 tests across the three ACP/spawn test files, including the Claude-specific cases for stripping model/thinking before initialise and avoiding first-turn replay of startup model/thinking.

- **What was not tested:** I did not replace/restart the full installed gateway from this PR branch during the PR steward pass. The live smoke proves the same managed-Claude-ACP failure mode is fixed in the running OpenClaw setup; the PR-head test run proves this branch contains the matching ACP/spawn regression coverage.
