target.title: feat: add tools.fs.roots — per-agent filesystem roots with access modes
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `tools.fs.workspaceOnly` is binary, so operators cannot safely allow agents to read or write a specific host path outside the workspace without opening broader filesystem access.
- Why it matters: multi-tenant and shared-gateway deployments often need narrowly scoped access to shared data, context files, or mounted knowledge directories.
- What changed: added `tools.fs.roots`, an explicit allowlist of filesystem roots with per-root `kind` (`dir`/`file`) and `access` (`ro`/`rw`), wired through host-mode FS tools, `apply_patch`, local media loading, outbound local-media/reply-delivery paths, and prompt-image auto-load.
- Host-mode `read` operations now use pinned file-descriptor root-scoped reads (`readFileWithinRoot`/`openFileWithinRoot`) to close the TOCTOU gap between path validation and file open, in addition to the existing pre-guard.
- Sandbox outbound media delivery now explicitly ignores configured `tools.fs.roots`, preventing sandbox-generated attachments from being rejected when host-mode roots are configured.
- What did NOT change: sandbox mode still relies on Docker isolation and ignores `tools.fs.roots`; `exec` sandboxing is unchanged; `tools.allow` / `tools.deny` behavior is unchanged.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable)

- Root cause: existing FS policy exposed only two modes for host paths: workspace-only or unrestricted.
- Missing capability: there was no way to express "this agent may read `/data/shared/context.md` but may not read the rest of the host".
- Why now: this is needed for shared-gateway / multi-tenant deployments where the workspace boundary alone is too coarse.
- If unknown, what was ruled out: sandbox mode stays out of scope because sandbox tools resolve container paths, not host paths, and partial host-path enforcement there would create an inconsistent security model.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should catch this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target tests/files:
  - `src/agents/tool-fs-policy.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/pi-tools.multi-root-guard.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/pi-tools.sandbox-mounted-paths.workspace-only.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/pi-tools.workspace-only-false.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/apply-patch.test.ts`
  - `src/media/local-roots.test.ts`
  - `src/media/load-options.test.ts`
  - `src/media/local-media-access.test.ts`
  - `src/auto-reply/reply/reply-media-paths.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/images.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/tools/image-tool.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/tools/image-generate-tool.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/tools/pdf-tool.test.ts`
  - `src/infra/outbound/deliver.test.ts`
  - `src/infra/fs-safe.test.ts`
  - `src/infra/fs-pinned-write-helper.spawn-error.test.ts`
  - `src/infra/fs-safe.unlink-helper-error.test.ts`
  - `extensions/googlechat/src/actions.test.ts`
  - `extensions/googlechat/src/channel.test.ts`
  - `extensions/feishu/src/media.test.ts`
  - `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/reply-delivery.test.ts`
- Scenarios locked in:
  - lexical dir/file root matching and ro/rw enforcement
  - canonical-root precedence over alias roots for host-mode mutation paths
  - unlink/delete behavior preserving symlink-entry semantics while still respecting stricter canonical roots
  - roots precedence over `workspaceOnly` for host-mode FS access
  - sandbox-mode fallback still honoring `workspaceOnly`
  - rooted local image/PDF/reference-image loads outside the workspace
  - rooted outbound local-media and reply-delivery paths preserving file-vs-dir root semantics
  - rooted prompt-image auto-load outside the workspace
  - host-mode reads use pinned-FD root-scoped operations (TOCTOU-safe)
  - sandboxed outbound media delivery ignores configured host roots
  - rooted delete helper gracefully falls back when pinned write helper spawn fails
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the risky surfaces here are policy resolution, host-path mutation, and media path propagation, so targeted seam tests catch the actual failure modes without requiring a full live deployment.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- New config surface: `tools.fs.roots` on global `tools.fs` and per-agent `tools.fs`.
- Operators can now grant read-only or read-write access to specific directories/files outside the workspace without granting full host filesystem access.
- Host-mode `read` / `write` / `edit` / `apply_patch` now respect those explicit roots.
- Local media loading, outbound local-media/reply-delivery flows, and prompt-image auto-load now preserve and enforce rooted file/dir access semantics.
- When both `roots` and `workspaceOnly` are set, `roots` takes precedence for host-mode FS access and a warning is logged.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:
  - This adds a new operator-controlled path restriction model for host-mode FS access. Risk is policy misunderstanding or incomplete enforcement across FS-adjacent surfaces. Mitigation: all configured roots are validated as absolute paths; enforcement is alias-safe; canonical root precedence is preserved for host-mode mutation paths; host-mode `read`/`write`/`edit`/`apply_patch`, local media inputs, outbound local-media/reply-delivery, and prompt-image auto-load are covered; host-mode reads use pinned-FD root-scoped operations to close TOCTOU gaps; sandbox mode explicitly ignores roots to avoid host/container path confusion.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS 15 / darwin 25.3.0
- Runtime/container: local repo checkout
- Model/provider: N/A for core FS policy; targeted local media tests use mocked image/PDF/image-generation providers
- Integration/channel (if any): host-mode FS tools + local media loading + outbound local-media reply flows
- Relevant config (redacted):

```json5
{
  "tools": {
    "fs": {
      "roots": [
        { "path": "/opt/app/workspaces/agent-1", "kind": "dir", "access": "rw" },
        { "path": "/data/finance/invoices", "kind": "dir", "access": "ro" },
        { "path": "/data/finance/context.md", "kind": "file", "access": "ro" }
      ]
    }
  }
}
```

### Steps

1. Configure an agent with `tools.fs.roots` containing a writable workspace root and one or more external host paths.
2. Attempt `read`/`write`/`edit`/`apply_patch` and local media access both inside and outside those roots.
3. Attempt outbound local-media / reply-delivery flows with both dir roots and exact file roots.
4. Attempt prompt-image auto-load from a rooted external image path.
5. Attempt the same config in sandbox mode.

### Expected

- Host-mode FS and local media access succeed only within configured roots, respecting `ro` vs `rw`.
- Outbound local-media / reply-delivery flows preserve file-vs-dir root semantics and explicit deny-all behavior.
- Prompt-image auto-load accepts explicitly rooted host paths.
- Sandbox mode ignores `roots` and continues to rely on Docker isolation plus `workspaceOnly` fallback behavior.

### Actual

- Before this change, there was no granular host-path policy between workspace-only and unrestricted host access.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified on the current merged head (`7b771d448d`):
  - merged `origin/main` at `631552c554`
  - `pnpm config:docs:gen`
  - `pnpm plugin-sdk:api:gen`
  - `pnpm check`
  - `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 pnpm test src/agents/pi-tools.workspace-only-false.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.memory-flush-forwarding.test.ts`
  - `pnpm test src/media/read-capability.test.ts`
  - `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 pnpm test src/media/read-capability.test.ts src/media/local-roots.test.ts`
  - `pnpm test extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply/monitor/inbound-dispatch.test.ts`
  - `pnpm test src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.spawn-workspace.context-engine.test.ts -t "sends transcriptPrompt visibly and queues runtime context as hidden custom context"`
  - `pnpm test src/agents/pi-tools.sandbox-mounted-paths.workspace-only.test.ts -t "ignores host roots for sandboxed read/write/edit tools"`
- Additional focused regressions were exercised during review for:
  - fs-root resolution / precedence
  - alias-safe host mutations and unlink/delete behavior
  - media root propagation and explicit deny-all handling
  - prompt-image and local-media rooted access
  - outbound local-media / reply-delivery adapters
  - pinned-FD host reads (TOCTOU closure)
  - sandbox outbound media ignoring configured roots
- What you did **not** verify on `7b771d448d`:
  - full local `pnpm test`
  - `pnpm build`
  - live end-to-end multi-tenant deployment behavior beyond local seam tests
  - `pnpm check:changed` did not complete locally: it passed type/lint/guard phases and 2,450 tests before a local Vitest gateway worker hit `ERR_WORKER_OUT_OF_MEMORY`

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: remove `tools.fs.roots` from config or revert this PR.
- Files/config to restore: `src/agents/pi-tools.ts`, `src/agents/tool-fs-policy.ts`, `src/agents/pi-tools.multi-root-guard.ts`, `src/agents/apply-patch.ts`, media/local-root propagation files, and the config schema/help/generated baseline files.
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: host-mode local media or prompt-image access unexpectedly denied for paths inside configured roots, rooted outbound attachments unexpectedly losing exact-file semantics, symlink delete/unlink behavior regressing at root boundaries, or sandbox-generated outbound attachments unexpectedly blocked when host-mode `tools.fs.roots` is configured.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: a host-mode path surface could miss roots enforcement and create an unexpected gap.
  - Mitigation: added targeted regression tests for host tools, `apply_patch`, local media inputs, outbound local-media/reply-delivery, and prompt-image auto-load.
- Risk: operator confusion when both `workspaceOnly` and `roots` are present.
  - Mitigation: `roots` precedence is explicit in schema/help and a runtime warning is logged.
- Risk: `exec` still bypasses FS roots through shell commands.
  - Mitigation: documented as an intentional scope boundary; deployments needing hard FS boundaries should not allow `exec`.

## AI assistance

- [x] AI-assisted
- Testing: locally revalidated on the current merged head (`7b771d448d`) with `pnpm check`, focused media/WhatsApp review-thread regressions, and the two prior PR-specific filtered tests; full `pnpm test` and `pnpm build` were not rerun on this final merged head; GitHub CI is green on `7b771d448d` (74 successful, 9 skipped) after the latest review fix.




## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** `tools.fs.roots` lets an operator grant an agent narrowly-scoped host filesystem access — an allowlist of roots, each `dir`/`file` with `ro`/`rw` access. Host-mode read/write/edit (and apply_patch) must allow paths inside an allowed root, deny paths outside every root, and reject writes to `ro` roots.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout at PR head `b4056fc332` (Node 22, macOS), driving the production host-mode filesystem tools against a real on-disk temporary directory tree (real `fs` syscalls; no fixtures or stubs).
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Created a real temp tree (an `rw` root `shared-rw`, an `ro` root `shared-ro`, and a `secret/` directory outside every root with a real file in it), built the production tool set via `createOpenClawCodingTools({ config: { tools: { fs: { roots: [...] } } } })`, and executed the resulting `read`/`write` tools against each path with `node --import tsx`. Live console output below.
- **Evidence after fix:**

```text
config tools.fs.roots:
  - { path: /tmp/fs-roots-proof-qTDt3u/shared-rw, kind: dir, access: rw }
  - { path: /tmp/fs-roots-proof-qTDt3u/shared-ro, kind: dir, access: ro }
workspace (not a root): /tmp/fs-roots-proof-qTDt3u/workspace
forbidden dir (not a root): /tmp/fs-roots-proof-qTDt3u/secret

=== host-mode reads ===
READ  inside rw root  (shared-rw/notes.txt)
      -> ALLOWED, content: "rw-root content"
READ  inside ro root  (shared-ro/policy.txt)
      -> ALLOWED, content: "ro-root content"
READ  OUTSIDE roots   (secret/passwords.txt)
      -> DENIED: Access denied: path '/tmp/fs-roots-proof-qTDt3u/secret/passwords.txt' is outside allowed filesystem roots

=== host-mode writes ===
WRITE inside rw root  (shared-rw/created.txt)
      -> ALLOWED, on disk: true
WRITE inside ro root  (shared-ro/hack.txt)
      -> DENIED: Access denied: path '/tmp/fs-roots-proof-qTDt3u/shared-ro/hack.txt' is inside read-only root '/tmp/fs-roots-proof-qTDt3u/shared-ro'  (on disk: false)
WRITE OUTSIDE roots   (secret/exfil.txt)
      -> DENIED: Access denied: path '/tmp/fs-roots-proof-qTDt3u/secret/exfil.txt' is outside allowed filesystem roots  (on disk: false)

verify: secret/passwords.txt still says: "TOP SECRET — outside every allowed root"
```

- **Observed result after fix:** Reads inside the `rw` and `ro` roots returned the real file contents. A read of `secret/passwords.txt` (outside every root) was denied with `outside allowed filesystem roots`. A write inside the `rw` root landed on disk (`on disk: true`); a write to the `ro` root was denied (`inside read-only root`) and a write outside every root was denied — both left nothing on disk (`on disk: false`), and the out-of-root secret file was byte-for-byte unchanged afterward.
- **What was not tested:** Sandbox mode is out of scope by design — sandbox ignores `tools.fs.roots` and relies on Docker isolation — so it was not exercised here. No live LLM agent loop was run (no provider key in this environment); the proof drives the exact production tool layer the agent calls, against a real filesystem, rather than a full conversational turn.

target.title: feat(pairing): add configurable pairingMessage text per channel (#41058)
target.body:
## Summary

Adds optional `pairingMessage` config to iMessage and Telegram channel configs, allowing operators to customize or suppress OpenClaw branding in pairing challenge messages sent to unknown senders.

Fixes #41058

## Problem

When `dmPolicy: pairing` is set, every unknown sender receives a hardcoded message exposing:
1. The OpenClaw brand name
2. The channel platform ("Your iMessage sender id:" / "Your Telegram user id:")
3. The full CLI invocation syntax (`openclaw pairing approve imessage CODE`)

No config option exists to override any part of this. The only workaround is patching compiled dist bundles after every update — not sustainable for white-label or privacy-conscious deployments.

## What changed

- `src/pairing/pairing-messages.ts`: Add `PairingMessageConfig` type with optional fields (`header`, `senderIdLabel`, `codeLabel`, `footer`, `showCliHint`). Update `buildPairingReply` to use config when provided.
- `src/config/types.imessage.ts`: Add `pairingMessage?: PairingMessageConfig`
- `src/config/types.telegram.ts`: Add `pairingMessage?: PairingMessageConfig`
- `src/config/zod-schema.providers-core.ts`: Add `PairingMessageConfigSchema`, wire into `IMessageAccountSchemaBase` and `TelegramAccountSchemaBase`
- `src/imessage/monitor/monitor-provider.ts`: Wire `pairingMessage` config into `issuePairingChallenge` via existing `buildReplyText` hook
- `src/telegram/dm-access.ts`: Pass `pairingMessage` through `enforceTelegramDmAccess`, wire into `issuePairingChallenge`
- `src/pairing/pairing-messages.test.ts`: Add tests for all config overrides and backward compat

## Fully backward compatible

All `PairingMessageConfig` fields are optional. Existing deployments with no `pairingMessage` config see zero behavior change.

## Example config

```yaml
channels:
  imessage:
    pairingMessage:
      header: 'Sender verification required.'
      senderIdLabel: 'Your sender ID:'
      footer: 'Share this code with your contact to complete verification.'
      showCliHint: false
```

## Notes

Discord, Signal, Slack, and LINE call sites follow the same pattern and can be extended in a follow-up if the team prefers to land iMessage/Telegram first.

## AI Disclosure
This PR was authored with AI assistance (OpenClaw/Jordan + Neo agents).
Testing: Fully tested — unit tests in `pairing-messages.test.ts` cover all config branches including edge cases; CI passes (types, lint, full test suite on Linux/Windows/macOS).
The authors have reviewed and understand all code changes in this PR.

target.title: fix(hooks): deliver internal hook replies on replyable surfaces
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `event.messages` was documented as a generic user-reply channel, but only `/new` and `/reset` actually drained those messages.
- Why it matters: `message:received` hooks could push reply text that was silently dropped, while other lifecycle hooks looked supported even though they had no safe reply target.
- What changed: added a shared internal hook reply-drain helper, kept reset/new command behavior, and now deliver `message:received` hook replies when a routable reply target exists.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this does not make bootstrap, preprocessed/transcribed, sent, or other lifecycle-only hooks auto-reply.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #39688
- Related #40185

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Internal hook replies pushed to `event.messages` are now delivered on `message:received` when OpenClaw has a reply-capable routing target.
- Docs now explicitly limit `event.messages` auto-delivery to reply-capable surfaces instead of implying it works for all hooks.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`No`)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`)
- New/changed network calls? (`No`)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`No`)
- Data access scope changed? (`No`)
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node 22 / pnpm workspace
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): internal hooks / inbound reply routing
- Relevant config (redacted): default hook + routeReply test harnesses

### Steps

1. Register an internal hook that pushes text into `event.messages`.
2. Trigger it from `/new` or `message:received`.
3. Verify the reply is routed only when a safe reply target exists.

### Expected

- Reply-capable surfaces route the accumulated hook messages.
- Lifecycle-only surfaces remain non-replying unless they gain an explicit delivery contract later.

### Actual

- Before this fix, `message:received` hook replies were silently dropped.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: `commands-core` still routes reset/new hook replies; `dispatch-from-config` now routes `message:received` hook replies on routable surfaces and skips non-routable ones.
- Edge cases checked: non-routable surfaces do not auto-send hook replies; docs/comments now match that contract.
- What you did **not** verify: live messaging-provider delivery outside the existing routeReply test harnesses.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`)
- Config/env changes? (`No`)
- Migration needed? (`No`)
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: revert `fix(hooks): deliver internal hook replies on replyable surfaces`
- Files/config to restore: `src/auto-reply/reply/internal-hook-replies.ts`, `src/auto-reply/reply/commands-core.ts`, `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.ts`, `docs/automation/hooks.md`
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: duplicate or unexpected hook replies on inbound turns, or `message:received` hook replies not routing when they should.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: `message:received` hook replies use the same route-reply path as other provider-routed responses, so a bad target selection would misroute the hook message.
  - Mitigation: the helper uses existing inbound routing fields (`OriginatingTo`, `To`, channel normalization) and adds explicit tests for routable and non-routable surfaces.
- Risk: this branch also carries a whitespace-only `CONTRIBUTING.md` formatter cleanup required for `pnpm check`.
  - Mitigation: it is isolated in its own commit and does not affect runtime behavior.

target.title: fix(heartbeat): clear pendingFinalDelivery* on send success
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #83184.

The heartbeat send path in `src/infra/heartbeat-runner.ts:2011-2022` wrote `lastHeartbeatText` / `lastHeartbeatSentAt` after a successful `sendDurableMessageBatch` but never nulled the `pendingFinalDelivery*` recovery fields. A heartbeat-driven agent run that previously set `pendingFinalDelivery: true` (via `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.ts:2169`) therefore left the session permanently stuck: subsequent heartbeats short-circuited in get-reply's redelivery path, the agent was never re-invoked, and the symptom surfaced as silent loss of notifications.

This change extends the existing post-success `updateSessionStore` block to clear all eight `pendingFinalDelivery*` fields, mirroring the canonical `clearPendingFinalDeliveryAfterSuccess` helper at `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.ts:365-391` (already invoked from the user-message dispatch path at `:1671`).

Affected versions: verified on `2026.5.7` through `2026.5.16-beta.2`; fix applies against current `main` (`2026.5.17`).

## Update — addressing ClawSweeper review

ClawSweeper flagged that the shared `CLEARED_PENDING_FINAL_DELIVERY_FIELDS` set cleared seven of the eight current `pendingFinalDelivery*` fields, omitting `pendingFinalDeliveryIntentId` — which the canonical `clearPendingFinalDeliveryAfterSuccess` does clear and which `SessionEntry` (`src/config/sessions/types.ts:353`) defines as durable pending-final metadata. A delivered heartbeat therefore left stale intent metadata behind, inconsistent with the dispatch path the helper claims to mirror.

Fixed by adding `pendingFinalDeliveryIntentId: undefined` to the constant (`src/infra/heartbeat-runner.ts`), which both heartbeat clear sites (send-success and duplicate-skip) already spread, so the heartbeat set now matches the canonical eight-field cleanup exactly. Both regression cases now seed `pendingFinalDeliveryIntentId` and assert it is cleared. Re-verified locally: removing only the new constant line turns both cases RED (`expected 'intent-send-success' to be undefined`, `expected 'intent-duplicate-skip' to be undefined`); restored, the file and ClawSweeper's listed acceptance-criteria siblings (3 files / 57 tests) pass; `oxfmt --check` and oxlint clean on both touched files.

The live Docker trace under *Real behavior proof* below predates this addition, so its bundle grep and observations describe the original seven `…: void 0` lines.

## Verification

Commands run locally against this branch on Ubuntu 24.04, Node 22.22.2, pnpm 11.1.0:

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/heartbeat-runner.clears-pending-final-delivery.test.ts` — new regression test, 1 passed (also confirmed RED before the fix landed in the same branch).
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs <all 16 src/infra/heartbeat-runner.*.test.ts non-e2e files>` — 16 files / 152 tests passed.
- `pnpm check:changed` — green: conflict markers, changelog attributions, wildcard re-export guards, duplicate coverage, dependency pin guard, package patch guard, typecheck core, typecheck core tests, lint core (0/0), media download helper guard, runtime sidecar loader guard, runtime import cycles, webhook body guard, pairing store guard, pairing account guard.
- `oxfmt --check` and core oxlint — both clean on the two touched files.

The regression file covers two cases. The send-success case seeds five `pendingFinalDelivery*` fields via the store seeder plus a heartbeat-ack `pendingFinalDeliveryText` (so the heartbeat-defer check at `heartbeat-runner.ts:~1328` does not short-circuit), patches in the three the seeder does not expose (`pendingFinalDeliveryLastAttemptAt`, `pendingFinalDeliveryContext`, `pendingFinalDeliveryIntentId`), runs `runHeartbeatOnce` with a substantive reply, and asserts all eight fields are `undefined` afterward — plus that `lastHeartbeatText` / `lastHeartbeatSentAt` are written, proving the success branch ran. The duplicate-skip case asserts the same eight-field clear when a `responsePrefix`-prefixed payload was already delivered, so the run short-circuits in the duplicate-suppression branch without a new send.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: heartbeat send-success path leaves `pendingFinalDelivery*` set, causing the next heartbeat to skip the agent via the get-reply redelivery short-circuit.

Real environment tested: Docker-deployed gateway on Ubuntu 24.04 (kernel 6.8.0-111-generic). Prior to this patch the gateway was on `OpenClaw 2026.5.12`; the bug was deterministic there, confirmed by an out-of-band cron job (`*/10 * * * *`) that nulls the stuck fields whenever `pendingFinalDeliveryText` matches `lastHeartbeatText`. Cron log baseline showed one "cleared stuck" entry per hour (e.g., `2026-05-17T15:50:01Z` and `2026-05-17T16:50:01Z`, both `agent:main:main`) — i.e., every substantive heartbeat left the session stuck and the cron had to clean up.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
1. Checked out this branch (`fix/heartbeat-clear-pending-final-delivery`, current `main` + the diff in this PR; reported `OpenClaw 2026.5.17`).
2. Built the openclaw image from this branch: `DOCKER_BUILDKIT=1 docker build -t openclaw-source:fix-heartbeat-pending-delivery .`.
3. Pointed the deployment stack at that locally built image and redeployed: `docker compose build && docker compose down && docker compose up -d`. New container came up at `2026-05-17T17:04:33Z`.
4. Confirmed the cleanup block compiled into the runtime bundle: `docker compose exec openclaw-gateway grep -c "pendingFinalDeliveryContext: void 0" /app/dist/heartbeat-runner-*.js` → `1` in the send-path bundle (`heartbeat-runner-CecDa3Rk.js`), `0` in the runtime stub (`heartbeat-runner-DPGtQP6X.js`). Context grep around `lastHeartbeatSentAt: startedAt` shows the seven `pendingFinalDelivery*: void 0` lines immediately after it, in the same `updateSessionStore` write that records `lastHeartbeatText`/`lastHeartbeatSentAt`.
5. Watched `agent:main:main` in the host-mounted `sessions.json` and the cron log over the next ~2 hours of real heartbeats.

Evidence after fix:
- New regression test (`src/infra/heartbeat-runner.clears-pending-final-delivery.test.ts`) goes RED on `main` (`expected true to be undefined` at the `pendingFinalDelivery` assertion) and GREEN with this patch. Sister tests in `src/infra/heartbeat-runner.*` (16 files / 152 tests) remain green.
- Live prod heartbeat trace (`agent:main:main`, host-mounted sessions store, redacted preview):
  - `2026-05-17T17:04:33Z` — patched container started (`OpenClaw 2026.5.17`).
  - `2026-05-17T17:04:41Z` — heartbeat scheduler logged `heartbeat: started intervalMs=1800000`. Subsequent ticks ran but were all HEARTBEAT_OK (silent, no store write), keeping `lastHeartbeatSentAt` at the pre-redeploy `2026-05-17T16:40:18Z` for ~2.5 hours.
  - `2026-05-17T19:10:18Z` — first substantive heartbeat after the redeploy. `lastHeartbeatSentAt` advanced to `1779045018769`, `lastHeartbeatText` written (`"One mildly notable item — everything else is promos: …"`).
  - `2026-05-17T19:11:04Z` (≤46s after the send write, well before the next `*/10` cron tick at `19:20:00Z`) — `pendingFinalDelivery: None`, `pendingFinalDeliveryText: ""`. This clean state is attributable only to the patched send-path write, not to the cron.
  - `2026-05-17T19:20:00Z` cron tick — zero new lines in `clear-stuck-pending-delivery.log` (baseline was 2 lines, post-tick was still 2 lines). On the unpatched image this is the tick that would have logged a "cleared stuck" line, matching the prior `15:50` / `16:50` cadence.

Observed result after fix: a real substantive heartbeat in the deployed gateway now leaves all seven `pendingFinalDelivery*` fields cleared in the same `updateSessionStore` write that records `lastHeartbeatText`/`lastHeartbeatSentAt`, and the standing cron mitigation no longer finds anything to clean up.

What was not tested:
- Multi-hour soak: only one substantive heartbeat has been observed post-deploy at the time of writing; a longer window would harden confidence that the cron stays silent across many cycles.
- Channels other than Telegram: this deployment only uses Telegram for heartbeat delivery, so WhatsApp / Slack send-success paths have not been exercised end-to-end (the unit test covers the post-success store write generically via the test-runtime channel plugin).
- The `pendingFinalDelivery: true` recovery branch where final delivery failed and a retry is genuinely needed — that path is still gated by the `clearPendingFinalDeliveryAfterSuccess` guard in `dispatch-from-config.ts`; this PR only adds the equivalent clear in the heartbeat send-success path.

target.title: fix(agents): auto-correct hallucinated document extensions in tool path params
target.body:
## Summary

- Models sometimes hallucinate document extensions by conflating Office file types with the "codex" token (`.docodex`, `.pptcodex`, `.xlscodex` instead of `.docx`, `.pptx`, `.xlsx`).
- These corrupted paths pass through the existing `wrapToolParamValidation` pipeline unchanged, causing file-tool operations (`read`, `write`, `edit`) to fail against non-existent filenames.
- This PR adds `normalizeHallucinatedDocumentExtension()` to detect and auto-correct these patterns, integrated into the shared validation pipeline alongside the existing XML suffix stripping.
- Covers all three file tools: write and edit via `wrapToolParamValidation`, read via `createOpenClawReadTool` (which has its own param normalization).
- Semantic choice: **silent auto-correct** — the hallucinated suffix is always a model error and never intentional; silently correcting avoids unnecessary retry overhead and lets the tool succeed on the real filename.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix / [ ] Feature / [ ] Refactor / [ ] Docs / [ ] Security / [ ] Chore

## Scope

- [ ] Gateway / [ ] Skills / [ ] Auth / [ ] Memory / [ ] Integrations / [x] API / [ ] UI/UX / [ ] CI

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** Models hallucinate `.docodex`/`.pptcodex`/`.xlscodex` extensions in tool call path parameters; these pass through validation unchanged and cause file operations to fail against non-existent filenames.

**Environment tested:** Node v24.13.1 on Linux, openclaw source at commit 70d8c7e4

**Steps run after the patch:**

```bash
node --import tsx --no-warnings -e "
import { normalizeHallucinatedDocumentExtension, wrapToolParamValidation, REQUIRED_PARAM_GROUPS } from './src/agents/agent-tools.params.js';
const execute = async (_id, args) => args;
const writeTool = wrapToolParamValidation({ name: 'write', label: 'write', description: 'write', parameters: {}, execute }, REQUIRED_PARAM_GROUPS.write);
const editTool = wrapToolParamValidation({ name: 'edit', label: 'edit', description: 'edit', parameters: {}, execute }, REQUIRED_PARAM_GROUPS.edit);
const readTool = wrapToolParamValidation({ name: 'read', label: 'read', description: 'read', parameters: {}, execute }, REQUIRED_PARAM_GROUPS.read);
const [wr, er, rr] = await Promise.all([
  writeTool.execute('id', { path: 'report.docodex', content: 'data' }),
  editTool.execute('id', { path: 'slides.pptcodex', edits: [{ oldText: 'a', newText: 'b' }] }),
  readTool.execute('id', { path: 'budget.xlscodex' }),
]);
console.log(JSON.stringify({ write: wr.path, edit: er.path, read: rr.path }, null, 2));
"
```

**Evidence after fix:**

Before (upstream/main — bug present, write path only; read/edit same behavior):

```text
write .docodex → report.docodex  (hallucinated extension passes through unchanged)
```

After (fix branch — all three tool types corrected):

```text
{
  "write": "report.docx",
  "edit": "slides.pptx",
  "read": "budget.xlsx"
}
```

**Observed result after the fix:** All three file tools (read, write, edit) auto-correct `.docodex`/`.pptcodex`/`.xlscodex` to `.docx`/`.pptx`/`.xlsx` before execution. Normal extensions pass through unchanged.

**What was not tested:** Live gateway session with a real model producing hallucinated extensions; shell-command paths (the fix only addresses structured file-tool path parameters, not exec tool commands).

## Root Cause

- Root cause: Models conflate Office extensions with the "codex" token during tool-call generation, producing non-existent file extensions.
- Missing detection/guardrail: The `wrapToolParamValidation` pipeline and `createOpenClawReadTool` stripped XML suffix corruption but had no guard against hallucinated document extensions.

## Regression Test Plan

- Target: `src/agents/agent-tools.params.test.ts` — `normalizeHallucinatedDocumentExtension` unit tests covering all three patterns, case-insensitivity, edge cases.
- Target: `src/agents/agent-tools.params.test.ts` — `wrapToolParamValidation with hallucinated document extension normalization` integration tests covering write, edit, read, XML-suffix and extension combo, and no-change paths.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Users will see `.docodex`/`.pptcodex`/`.xlscodex` paths in tool calls silently corrected to `.docx`/`.pptx`/`.xlsx`, allowing file operations to succeed against the intended filenames.

## Security Impact

- New permissions? No
- Secrets handling changed? No
- New network calls? No

Closes #93326

target.title: fix(session): clear per-channel origin fields on provider change (fixes #95325)
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

When `session.dmScope: "main"` is configured and a user messages the same agent across different channels (e.g. Slack DM then Telegram DM), the session's `origin.nativeChannelId` retains the previous channel's ID because `mergeOrigin` only overwrites fields when the new inbound explicitly supplies them. Telegram DMs don't supply `nativeChannelId`, so the stale Slack channel ID persists indefinitely until the user messages on the original channel again.

This affects any cross-channel session metadata — `nativeChannelId`, `nativeDirectUserId`, `threadId`, and `accountId` can all go stale.

## Fix

In `mergeOrigin` (`src/config/sessions/metadata.ts`), when the provider changes, clear per-channel identity fields before applying new values. Cross-platform fields (`label`, `from`, `to`, `surface`, `chatType`) are preserved.

- 1 source file changed (+11 lines)

target.title: feat(embedded-runner): expose prep stage timings
target.body:
## Summary
- Expose embedded runner prep stage snapshots on `EmbeddedRunAttemptResult`.
- Include the same prep stage summary on the `agent_end` hook event for plugin observability.
- Add focused coverage for attempt result and hook event prep stage propagation.
- Document the new optional hook field. No `CHANGELOG.md` change is kept.

## Current upstream status
Latest `origin/main` already has May embedded-runner/subagent performance work and trace logging for startup stages. It still does not expose the attempt prep stage summary to `agent_end` hook observers. This PR is narrowed to that missing diagnostics surface so plugins can collect stage timing evidence without patching core locally.

## Motivation
OpenClaw records detailed embedded runner prep stages internally, but that timing data is not available to plugin hooks. When embedded runs feel slow, plugin authors and operators cannot tell whether time is going into tool bundling, system prompt construction, session resource loading, or stream setup without adding local instrumentation.

Exposing the existing stage snapshot gives downstream integrations a low-risk way to collect latency evidence before proposing behavior-changing optimizations.

## Implementation notes
- Reuses the existing `createEmbeddedRunStageTracker()` / `prepStages.snapshot()` path.
- Keeps all new fields optional.
- Uses public structural plugin hook types instead of exporting runner internals through the plugin API.
- Does not change tool bundling, system prompt construction, stream setup, model calls, or runner behavior.
- Documents `agent_end.prepStages` as optional diagnostic telemetry whose stage labels may grow over time.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Embedded runner prep stage timings are visible to a real non-bundled plugin's `agent_end` hook without changing the embedded run outcome.

Real environment tested: macOS local OpenClaw source checkout, current PR head `8d7ef409272853778bd234da21e777ac880216a6`, temporary `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, temporary `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH`, temporary local plugin loaded through `plugins.load.paths` with `hooks.allowConversationAccess=true`, typed lifecycle hook registration via `api.on("agent_end", ...)`, `node scripts/run-node.mjs agent --local --json --session-key agent:default:prep-proof`, and the repo mock OpenAI-compatible server `scripts/e2e/mock-openai-server.mjs`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Started the mock OpenAI server with `SUCCESS_MARKER=OPENCLAW_PREP_PROOF_OK`, ran a local embedded agent turn against a temporary config, and had the temporary plugin write the real `agent_end` event to JSONL. The proof script asserted both the CLI result and plugin hook payload.

Fresh current-head verification:

```text
node --max-old-space-size=8192 --import tsx scripts/generate-plugin-sdk-api-baseline.ts --check
OK docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256

node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-embedded-agent.config.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.spawn-workspace.context-engine.test.ts -t "exposes embedded prep stages" --reporter=dot
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  1 passed | 58 skipped (59)
```

Evidence after fix: copied live terminal output from the current-head local OpenClaw CLI run and typed non-bundled plugin `agent_end` hook JSONL assertion.

```json
{
  "head": "8d7ef409272853778bd234da21e777ac880216a6",
  "finalAssistantVisibleText": "OPENCLAW_PREP_PROOF_OK",
  "hookSuccess": true,
  "hookHasPrepStages": true,
  "hookTotalMs": 143,
  "hookStageNames": [
    "workspace-sandbox",
    "skills",
    "core-plugin-tools",
    "bootstrap-context",
    "bundle-tools",
    "system-prompt",
    "session-resource-loader",
    "agent-session",
    "stream-setup"
  ],
  "proofDir": "/tmp/openclaw-78381-proof-current.Xjp9Fj"
}
```

Observed result after fix: A standalone embedded `openclaw agent --local` run completed successfully against the mock OpenAI server, returned `OPENCLAW_PREP_PROOF_OK`, and the temporary plugin's real typed `agent_end` handler observed `event.prepStages` with total setup time and expected prep stages.

What was not tested: A live paid external model/provider run was not run. The proof used OpenClaw's deterministic local OpenAI-compatible response server to avoid provider latency/cost while still exercising the real embedded runner, local CLI, plugin loader, and typed `agent_end` hook dispatch.

## Compatibility / Migration
- Backward compatible? `Yes`
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`
- Risk: plugin authors might treat stage names as permanent.
- Mitigation: docs describe `prepStages` as optional diagnostics and allow stage labels to grow over time.

target.title: fix(config): add stdio to McpServerSchema transport union
target.body:
## Summary

`openclaw config validate` rejects `transport: "stdio"` on command-bearing MCP servers, even though the runtime (`resolveMcpTransportConfig`) correctly resolves any server with a `command` field as stdio — creating an inconsistency between validator and runtime.

The fix adds `z.literal("stdio")` to the `transport` union in `McpServerSchema` (src/config/zod-schema.ts:395), so the schema matches what the runtime already accepts. The runtime's resolver already ignores the `transport` value when a `command` is present (it always returns `kind: "stdio"`), so this is purely a schema-side alignment.

**Change**: one line, one file.

Fixes #95082

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: `config validate` now accepts `transport: "stdio"` alongside `command` on an MCP server definition, matching the runtime behavior instead of producing a false-positive validation error.

**Real environment tested**: Ubuntu 24.04 (Linux 6.8.0-generic), Node v24, pnpm 11.2.2, OpenClaw build from current main

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
```bash
openclaw mcp set demo-stdio '{"command":"npx","args":["-y","@modelcontextprotocol/server-memory"],"transport":"stdio"}'
openclaw config validate
```

**After-fix evidence**:
```
$ node openclaw.mjs config validate
Config valid: ~/.openclaw-dev/openclaw.json
```

**Observed result after the fix**: `config validate` passes with `Config valid:` — no longer rejects the `"stdio"` transport value. The configured MCP server with `command` + `transport: "stdio"` is accepted by the validator.

**What was not tested**: Full runtime MCP connectivity (requires credentials/auth) — this is a config-schema-only change; the runtime resolver was already tested separately and unchanged.

## Tests and validation

### Unit tests

```
$ pnpm test -- src/agents/bundle-mcp-config.test.ts

 RUN  v4.1.8

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  4 passed (4)
   Start at  05:14:19
   Duration  813ms
```

### Build

```
$ pnpm build
✓ built in 244.3s
[build-all] phase timings: total 244.3s
```

### Real output: config validate before vs after

**Before** (without this patch — on current main):
The `transport` union only accepts `"sse" | "streamable-http"`, so a config with `transport: "stdio"` fails `config validate` with a Zod union error.

**After** (with this patch):
```
$ node openclaw.mjs config validate
Config valid: ~/.openclaw-dev/openclaw.json
```

### Diff
```diff
-    transport: z.union([z.literal("sse"), z.literal("streamable-http")]).optional(),
+    transport: z.union([z.literal("stdio"), z.literal("sse"), z.literal("streamable-http")]).optional(),
```

## Risk checklist

- [x] This change is backwards compatible — adds a new allowed value to an existing union without removing or changing any existing behavior.
- [x] This change has been tested with existing configurations — `config validate` passes on the baseline dev config before and after.
- [ ] I have updated relevant documentation — the docs already steer users to omit `transport` for stdio servers; the schema now accepts the explicit value for robustness.
- [ ] Breaking changes (if any) are documented in Summary — no breaking changes.

## Current review state

- [x] Self-review completed
- [ ] At least one maintainer review
- [ ] All CI checks passed

target.title: feat(ui): add generated image actions
target.body:
## Summary

Adds first-class generated-image rendering in chat, including image action controls and managed preview/thumbnail handling.

- Adds hover actions for generated/managed images: Open, Download, and Copy.
- Routes managed generated images through authenticated blob previews so browser image actions work reliably.
- Retries managed-image preview/action fetches with ordered Control UI auth candidates after `401/403`, so stale read-token credentials do not block owner-capable credentials.
- Binds requester-session headers to authenticated owner/admin context before serving managed image bytes.
- Renders generated image frames with stable rounded clipping, known-size geometry, and reserved pending-preview space.
- Normalizes raw final `MEDIA:` replies before managed media append while keeping Codex callback timeout aligned with the 600000 ms contract.
- Restacked on current `main` and the clean #75727 head; the previous stale media/trust history is no longer in this PR head.
- Removed the public `gateway.controlUi.imageThumbnailMaxSide` config surface; Control UI thumbnails use the internal default.

## Why

Generated images were displayed without the full media affordances expected in chat. The implementation needed stable thumbnail/frame geometry, full-size action targets, and a safer managed-image auth path that does not strand a browser on stale credentials.

## Real behavior proof

- Current head tested: `5fbacf09ca6a7b5bfea79c8f0970519603b874ff`.
- Behavior addressed: generated/managed chat images render as authenticated thumbnail previews, Open/Download/Copy use the full image, owner-capable credentials are retried after stale `401/403` credentials, and the public thumbnail-size config knob is gone.
- Real environment tested: local Linux checkout after restacking on current `main`, Vitest gateway/UI/config shards, and oxlint.

Commands run after the current fix:

```bash
git diff --check
rg -n "imageThumbnailMaxSide" src ui docs -S
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/replay-history.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.tools.media.test.ts src/config/config-misc.test.ts src/gateway/control-ui.http.test.ts src/gateway/managed-image-attachments.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/chat.directive-tags.test.ts ui/src/ui/chat/grouped-render.test.ts ui/src/ui/controllers/chat.test.ts ui/src/ui/app-render.helpers.node.test.ts --reporter=verbose
node scripts/run-oxlint-shards.mjs --threads=8
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs ui/src/ui/chat/grouped-render.test.ts --reporter=verbose
```

Evidence:

```text
$ rg -n "imageThumbnailMaxSide" src ui docs -S
# no matches

$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs ... --reporter=verbose
[test] passed 5 Vitest shards in 127.90s

$ node scripts/run-oxlint-shards.mjs --threads=8
[oxlint:core] finished
[oxlint:extensions] finished
[oxlint:scripts] finished

$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs ui/src/ui/chat/grouped-render.test.ts --reporter=verbose
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  61 passed (61)
[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 304.41s
```

Specific regression locked in:

- `ui/src/ui/chat/grouped-render.test.ts` now verifies managed-image thumbnail and full-image action fetches try `Bearer session-token`, receive `403`, then retry and succeed with `Bearer owner-token` while preserving `x-openclaw-requester-session-key`.
- `ui/src/ui/app-render.helpers.node.test.ts` verifies ordered unique auth candidates.
- `src/gateway/control-ui.http.test.ts` now verifies bounded thumbnail generation without exposing a public config key.

## Security Impact

- New permissions/capabilities? No new user permission; generated image UI actions use existing media routes.
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? Yes. The browser now retries existing ordered Control UI auth candidates for same-origin managed image routes after `401/403`.
- Data access scope changed? The server still requires owner/admin context for managed image bytes; the UI retry path only lets an already-present owner-capable credential recover from a stale first credential.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes.
- Config/env changes? No. The proposed public thumbnail-size config was removed before merge.
- Migration needed? No.

target.title: fix(agent-tools): correct hallucinated document file extensions in tool path parameters (#93326)
target.body:
## Summary

Add a narrow guard at the agent tool path boundary to detect known hallucinated document file extensions that models may produce (e.g. `.docx` rewritten as `.docodex`).

The fix uses two different strategies depending on the operation type:
- **Read tools**: silently correct the extension (safe — read-only, no mutation risk)
- **Write/Edit/Memory-flush tools**: reject the path with a retryable error and require the model to supply the correct extension (prevents accidental mutation of a different file)

Fixes #93326

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

**Behavior addressed**: When an OpenClaw agent tool receives a path parameter with a model-hallucinated file extension (e.g. `report.docodex` instead of `report.docx`):
- Read tools silently correct to `.docx` and proceed
- Write/edit tools reject the path and ask the model to retry with the correct extension

**Evidence type**: terminal

**Real setup tested**:
- **Runtime**: `v24.13.1`, `Linux 4.19.112-2.el8.x86_64`

**Exact test run after fix**:

```bash
$ pnpm test -- src/agents/agent-tools.params.test.ts src/agents/agent-tools.read.arg-value-suffix.test.ts
```

**After-fix evidence**:

```
 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  30 passed (30)
```

Including 14 new tests covering:
- `correctHallucinatedFileExtension` unit tests for all 4 known patterns
- Integration tests verifying write/edit tools **reject** hallucinated paths (not silent correction)
- Integration tests verifying read tool corrects silently

```bash
$ pnpm test -- src/agents/agent-tools.read.workspace-root-guard.test.ts src/agents/agent-tools.read.host-edit-access.test.ts src/agents/agent-tools.read.host-tilde-expansion.test.ts
```

```
 Test Files  3 passed (3)
      Tests  25 passed (25)
```

**Observed result after the fix**:

| Input | Write/Edit Tool | Read Tool |
|-------|----------------|-----------|
| `report.docodex` | ❌ Rejected — retryable error | ✅ Silently reads `report.docx` |
| `slides.pptcodex` | ❌ Rejected — retryable error | ✅ Silently reads `slides.pptx` |
| `report.docx` | ✅ Passes through unchanged | ✅ Passes through unchanged |

**What was not tested**: Shell command execution paths (`bash-tools.exec.ts`) are not modified since hallucinated path correction in free-form shell strings requires more robust parsing.

## Tests and validation

| Test Type | Result | Details |
|-----------|--------|---------|
| Unit Tests (params) | ✅ | 30/30 passed (14 new hallucination tests + 16 existing) |
| Read workspace-root-guard | ✅ | 17/17 passed |
| Read host-edit-access | ✅ | 4/4 passed |
| Read host-tilde-expansion | ✅ | 4/4 passed |
| Read arg-value-suffix | ✅ | 2/2 passed |

## Files changed

- **src/agents/agent-tools.params.ts** — Add `correctHallucinatedFileExtension()`, `correctHallucinatedExtensionsFromKeys()`, and `HALLUCINATED_EXTENSION_SUFFIXES` mapping. `wrapToolParamValidation` now **rejects** hallucinated extensions for write/edit tools
- **src/agents/agent-tools.read.ts** — Import and apply `correctHallucinatedFileExtension()`: read tool silently corrects; memory-flush write tool rejects
- **src/agents/agent-tools.params.test.ts** — Add 14 new tests covering patterns, read correction, write/edit rejection, and normal path passthrough

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`No`) — Normal paths are unaffected. Hallucinated paths are either corrected (read) or rejected with a clear error (write/edit).

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`No`)

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`No`)

What is the highest-risk area?
- Write/edit rejection: a model that consistently hallucinates `.docodex` would get persistent errors, but this is correct behavior — it forces the model to supply a valid path rather than silently mutating a wrong file.

How is that risk mitigated?
- The error is retryable with clear guidance ("Supply the correct file extension before retrying")
- The `requred params` validation layer returns actionable errors that agents can recover from

## Current review state

What is the next action?
- ClawSweeper re-review

commands.author: lzyyzznl

target.title: fix(config): add stdio to McpServerSchema transport union
target.body:
## Summary

`openclaw config validate` rejects `transport: "stdio"` on command-bearing MCP servers, even though the runtime (`resolveMcpTransportConfig`) correctly resolves any server with a `command` field as stdio — creating an inconsistency between validator and runtime.

The fix adds `z.literal("stdio")` to the `transport` union in `McpServerSchema` (src/config/zod-schema.ts:395), so the schema matches what the runtime already accepts. The runtime's resolver already ignores the `transport` value when a `command` is present (it always returns `kind: "stdio"`), so this is purely a schema-side alignment.

**Change**: one line, one file.

Fixes #95082

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: `config validate` now accepts `transport: "stdio"` alongside `command` on an MCP server definition, matching the runtime behavior instead of producing a false-positive validation error.

**Real environment tested**: Ubuntu 24.04 (Linux 6.8.0-generic), Node v24, pnpm 11.2.2, OpenClaw build from current main

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
```bash
openclaw mcp set demo-stdio '{"command":"npx","args":["-y","@modelcontextprotocol/server-memory"],"transport":"stdio"}'
openclaw config validate
```

**After-fix evidence**:
```
$ node openclaw.mjs config validate
Config valid: ~/.openclaw-dev/openclaw.json
```

**Observed result after the fix**: `config validate` passes with `Config valid:` — no longer rejects the `"stdio"` transport value. The configured MCP server with `command` + `transport: "stdio"` is accepted by the validator.

**What was not tested**: Full runtime MCP connectivity (requires credentials/auth) — this is a config-schema-only change; the runtime resolver was already tested separately and unchanged.

## Tests and validation

### Unit tests

```
$ pnpm test -- src/agents/bundle-mcp-config.test.ts

 RUN  v4.1.8

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  4 passed (4)
   Start at  05:14:19
   Duration  813ms
```

### Build

```
$ pnpm build
✓ built in 244.3s
[build-all] phase timings: total 244.3s
```

### Real output: config validate before vs after

**Before** (without this patch — on current main):
The `transport` union only accepts `"sse" | "streamable-http"`, so a config with `transport: "stdio"` fails `config validate` with a Zod union error.

**After** (with this patch):
```
$ node openclaw.mjs config validate
Config valid: ~/.openclaw-dev/openclaw.json
```

### Diff
```diff
-    transport: z.union([z.literal("sse"), z.literal("streamable-http")]).optional(),
+    transport: z.union([z.literal("stdio"), z.literal("sse"), z.literal("streamable-http")]).optional(),
```

## Risk checklist

- [x] This change is backwards compatible — adds a new allowed value to an existing union without removing or changing any existing behavior.
- [x] This change has been tested with existing configurations — `config validate` passes on the baseline dev config before and after.
- [ ] I have updated relevant documentation — the docs already steer users to omit `transport` for stdio servers; the schema now accepts the explicit value for robustness.
- [ ] Breaking changes (if any) are documented in Summary — no breaking changes.

## Current review state

- [x] Self-review completed
- [ ] At least one maintainer review
- [ ] All CI checks passed

commands.author: NianJiuZst

target.title: fix: preserve user text that looks like inbound metadata
target.body:
Closes #95279

## Summary

- **What did NOT change:** This does not remove legacy sentinel stripping for already-persisted rows, does not migrate transcript files, does not change active-turn model prompt construction, and does not add unrelated provider/channel routing behavior.
- **Why it matters / User impact:** Users who paste or type literal inbound-metadata-looking text should not lose that text from Control UI history, text extraction, memory session export, CLI imported-session dedupe, or historical LLM replay when OpenClaw already knows the canonical bare user body for a decorated inbound turn.
- **Behavior addressed:** user-authored text that looks like OpenClaw inbound metadata could be stripped or deduped incorrectly because downstream consumers inferred the bare body from forgeable sentinel text inside `content`.

## Why This Contract Shape

The accepted source of truth in this PR is a host-owned metadata slot under the existing OpenClaw-owned message bag: `__openclaw.inboundDecoration.bareBody`.

Why this shape instead of top-level `bareBody` / `inboundDecorated` fields:

- **Ownership stays explicit:** this data is OpenClaw-generated transport/runtime metadata, not user content and not a general message-schema field. Keeping it under `__openclaw` matches the repo's existing ownership pattern for OpenClaw-managed per-message metadata.
- **Narrower compatibility surface:** top-level fields would widen the generic message contract across more callers and plugin consumers. Nesting under `__openclaw.inboundDecoration` keeps the new state additive and clearly internal while still serializing it where the affected consumers already read OpenClaw metadata.
- **Room for the same family of trusted decoration data:** the `inboundDecoration` object can carry future trusted decoration facts without minting more one-off top-level keys.
- **Best-fit boundary for this fix:** the bug is not “messages in general need a new public bareBody field”; it is “OpenClaw needs one trusted place to remember the host-authored bare body for inbound-decorated user turns.”

## Real behavior proof

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
  - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/cli-session-history.test.ts src/sessions/user-turn-transcript.test.ts ui/src/ui/chat/message-extract.test.ts ui/src/ui/chat/message-normalizer.test.ts packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/session-files.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.llm-boundary.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/strip-inbound-meta.test.ts`
  - `git diff --check`
  - `node --import tsx <transcript-ui-proof>` using repository runtime code to persist a real transcript row with `appendUserTurnTranscriptMessage(...)`, then feed that persisted row through `normalizeMessage(...)` and `extractTextCached(...)`
  - `node --import tsx <cli-dedupe-proof>` using repository runtime code to call `mergeImportedChatHistoryMessages(...)` with two distinct trusted bare bodies that share decorated model-facing `content`
- **Observed result after fix:** targeted regression tests passed, `git diff --check` passed, the transcript/UI proof preserved literal `Sender (untrusted metadata):` user text when the trusted bare body was present, and the CLI imported-session dedupe proof kept two rows with different trusted bare bodies even though the legacy sentinel-stripped comparable text would have collided.
- **What was not tested:** I did not run an external live channel or packaged app proof in this environment. The real-behavior proof here exercises the repository runtime code locally with synthetic persisted transcript data and CLI merge inputs.
- **Real environment tested:** local source checkout on this branch using repository Vitest lanes and small `tsx` proof harnesses that import the production code paths directly.
- **Evidence after fix:** transcript persistence, Control UI display/extraction, CLI imported-session dedupe, memory-host export/indexing, and historical replay consumers all prefer the trusted bare body when present and fall back to legacy sentinel stripping only for unmarked legacy rows.

Transcript/UI proof output, redacted to synthetic data:

```text
Command shape:
node --import tsx <transcript-ui-proof>

{
  "trusted": {
    "normalizedText": "Sender (untrusted metadata):\n```json\n{\"label\":\"literal user-authored text\"}\n```\n\nPlease preserve this literal block.",
    "extractedText": "Sender (untrusted metadata):\n```json\n{\"label\":\"literal user-authored text\"}\n```\n\nPlease preserve this literal block.",
    "preservedLiteralSenderSentinel": true,
    "usedTrustedBareBodyInsteadOfDecoratedCopy": true
  },
  "legacyFallbackWithoutTrustedField": {
    "normalizedText": "Please preserve this literal block.",
    "extractedText": "Please preserve this literal block.",
    "strippedLiteralSenderSentinel": true
  }
}

Observed result: the same persisted decorated row keeps the literal sentinel-shaped user text when the trusted bare body exists, and the legacy fallback still strips that literal sentinel text when the trusted field is absent.
```

CLI imported-session dedupe proof output, redacted to synthetic data:

```text
Command shape:
node --import tsx <cli-dedupe-proof>

{
  "mergedWithTrustedContract": 2,
  "mergedWithoutTrustedContract": 1,
  "legacyComparableTextCollision": true,
  "preservedDistinctTrustedBareBodies": true
}

Observed result: with the trusted contract, the CLI merge path keeps both rows; without the trusted field, the legacy sentinel-strip comparable text collapses the same two rows into one.
```

## Regression Test Plan

- **Target test files:** `src/gateway/cli-session-history.test.ts`, `src/sessions/user-turn-transcript.test.ts`, `ui/src/ui/chat/message-extract.test.ts`, `ui/src/ui/chat/message-normalizer.test.ts`, `packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/session-files.test.ts`, `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.llm-boundary.test.ts`, and `src/auto-reply/reply/strip-inbound-meta.test.ts`.
- **Scenario locked in:** each consumer test covers a decorated inbound turn whose trusted bare body itself contains sentinel-shaped user text such as `Sender (untrusted metadata):` plus fenced JSON; the expected behavior is that the trusted bare body is preserved literally instead of being reparsed and stripped as metadata. The CLI imported-session test additionally proves two messages with identical decorated `content` but different trusted bare bodies do not dedupe into one row.
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:** it locks the shared trusted-contract behavior at the persistence, UI, CLI dedupe, memory, and replay boundaries without changing unrelated prompt-construction behavior or removing legacy fallback cleanup for old rows.

## Root Cause

- **Root cause:** the parser/contract boundary was wrong. `buildInboundUserContextPrefix(...)` creates host decoration as plaintext blocks inside user-message `content`, but consumers such as Control UI history, CLI imported-session dedupe, memory-host session export, and historical LLM replay had no trusted way to distinguish host-added decoration from user-authored text in that same channel.
- **Why this is the root-cause fix:** the source-of-truth decision no longer comes from forgeable `content` text. The affected consumers now prefer one shared trusted resolver and only fall back to legacy sentinel stripping for older rows that do not carry the trusted field.
- **Fix classification:** root-cause fix for a public session/transcript contract bug.
- **Maintainer-ready confidence:** high. The change is additive, preserves legacy fallback behavior, and has targeted regression coverage plus local real-behavior proof for the affected consumer paths.
- **Architecture / source-of-truth check:** the canonical contract is the host-owned per-message metadata under `__openclaw.inboundDecoration.bareBody`. Display/history/CLI dedupe/memory/replay consumers use that trusted contract when present; legacy unmarked rows still go through sentinel stripping.

## Scope boundary

In scope: trusted inbound bare-body metadata plumbing for persisted/runtime user turns, transcript persistence/runtime merge preservation, UI display normalization and text extraction, CLI imported-session dedupe, memory-host session export/indexing, historical LLM replay normalization, and targeted regression tests for those paths.

Out of scope: removing legacy sentinel stripping for already-persisted rows, migrating existing transcript files, changing active-turn model prompt construction, or broadening the generic message schema with new top-level public fields.

## Verification

Passed locally:

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/cli-session-history.test.ts src/sessions/user-turn-transcript.test.ts ui/src/ui/chat/message-extract.test.ts ui/src/ui/chat/message-normalizer.test.ts packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/session-files.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.llm-boundary.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/strip-inbound-meta.test.ts
```

```text
git diff --check
```

## Merge risk

- Risk labels considered: `compatibility`, `session-state`.
- Risk explanation: this changes display/replay/indexing/dedupe behavior for messages that explicitly opt into the trusted inbound-decoration contract. The new serialized state is additive and only authoritative when present; existing transcripts without the field keep the old heuristic fallback.
- Why acceptable: the fix narrows an unsafe heuristic without removing backward compatibility for legacy rows, and the proof covers the user-visible paths most likely to drop or dedupe real user-authored content.

target.title: fix(ui): show cron job model selection
target.body: Related: #93507 ## What Problem This Solves Fixes an issue where users creating cron jobs from the Web UI quick-create flow could not choose a model, and existing cron jobs did not make their configured/default model visible in the job list/details. ## Why This Change Was Made This reuses the existing cron `payload.model` / Control UI `payloadModel` contract instead of adding runtime or schema behavior. The quick-create wizard now accepts the same model suggestions already loaded for the advanced cron form, maps the selected value into the cron form patch, and scheduled agent jobs show either the configured model or an explicit default label in both the row chips and job detail section. ## User Impact Users can choose a model while creating a cron job from the guided Web UI flow, and can inspect scheduled agent jobs at a glance to see which model they will use. Jobs that use the default model now say so explicitly. ## Evidence - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs ui/src/ui/views/cron-quick-create.node.test.ts ui/src/ui/views/cron.test.ts ui/src/ui/controllers/cron.test.ts` passed: 3 files, 60 tests. - `git diff --check` passed. - `pnpm exec oxfmt --check ui/src/ui/app-render.ts ui/src/ui/views/cron-quick-create.ts ui/src/ui/views/cron.ts ui/src/ui/views/cron-quick-create.node.test.ts ui/src/ui/views/cron.test.ts` passed. - `python .agents\skills\autoreview\scripts\autoreview --mode branch --base origin/main` passed with `autoreview clean: no accepted/actionable findings reported`. - Mocked Control UI / Playwright proof passed with the real Vite Control UI, a mocked Gateway, and system Chrome. Proof JSON: https://gist.github.com/ly85206559/d24a74e8dd7818dc34c1da6ba7d66a24 - Playwright proof confirmed the existing cron row and detail both show `Model: openai/gpt-5.2` for an agentTurn job. - Playwright proof confirmed the quick-create How step accepts `openai/gpt-5.5` and the captured `cron.add` request includes `payload.model: openai/gpt-5.5`. - Playwright proof reported `pageErrors: []`. - Local visual artifacts generated for inspection: `.artifacts/control-ui-e2e/cron-model-proof/01-job-list-model.png`, `.artifacts/control-ui-e2e/cron-model-proof/02-quick-create-model.png`, and `.artifacts/control-ui-e2e/cron-model-proof/page@5d408e05bab85b27a18d6dd4d4094162.webm`.

commands.author: yetval

target.title: fix(skills): refuse owner-qualified ClawHub force install over a different owner
target.body:
## Summary
Installing an owner-qualified ClawHub skill with `--force` over a skill already installed under a different owner silently destroys the existing skill and rewrites its tracking to the new owner. With `@alice/weather` installed, running `openclaw skills install @bob/weather --force` reports success while replacing Alice's skill with Bob's under the same local name and flipping the lockfile/origin `ownerHandle` to `bob`. There is no way to keep both and no warning. The CLI itself recommends `--force` when a plain install errors with "already exists", so this is an easy path to data loss of installed state.

This was introduced by the just-landed owner-qualified install support in #94282 (ebd87e7f37).

## Root cause
The update path guards against owner mismatch, but the install path does not. In `resolveRequestedUpdateSlug` the update flow refuses to act when the requested owner differs from the tracked owner:

before (`src/skills/lifecycle/clawhub.ts`, update path only):
```ts
if (trackedOrigin || trackedLockEntry) {
  const trackedOwnerHandle = trackedOrigin?.ownerHandle ?? trackedLockEntry?.ownerHandle;
  if (requestedRef.ownerHandle && trackedOwnerHandle !== requestedRef.ownerHandle) {
    const trackedRef = trackedOwnerHandle ? `@${trackedOwnerHandle}/${trackedSlug}` : trackedSlug;
    throw new Error(
      `Skill "${trackedSlug}" is tracked as ${trackedRef}, not @${requestedRef.ownerHandle}/${trackedSlug}.`,
    );
  }
  return trackedSlug;
}
```

`performClawHubSkillInstall` derives `targetDir` and the lock/origin key from the bare `params.slug` with the owner dropped into `ownerHandle`, and has no equivalent guard. With `--force`, the `pathExists(targetDir)` check is skipped and `installPackageDir` does a backup-then-replace, so an `@bob/weather` install lands directly on top of the tracked `@alice/weather`.

## Fix
Extract the owner-match check into a shared `assertClawHubOwnerMatch` helper used by both the update path and the install path. The install path now reads the existing origin and lockfile entry before overwriting and refuses, even under `--force`, when the requested owner differs from the tracked owner:

after (`src/skills/lifecycle/clawhub.ts`, install path):
```ts
if (params.ownerHandle) {
  const trackedOrigin = await readClawHubSkillOrigin(targetDir);
  const trackedLockEntry = (await readClawHubSkillsLockfile(params.workspaceDir)).skills[
    params.slug
  ];
  if (trackedOrigin || trackedLockEntry) {
    assertClawHubOwnerMatch({
      slug: params.slug,
      requestedOwnerHandle: params.ownerHandle,
      trackedOwnerHandle: trackedOrigin?.ownerHandle ?? trackedLockEntry?.ownerHandle,
    });
  }
}
```

The guard only fires for owner-qualified requests against an already-tracked skill, so fresh installs and same-owner force reinstalls/updates are unaffected. The update flow keeps its identical behavior through the shared helper.

## Why this is the right boundary
The owner-mismatch invariant is one rule that belongs to both lifecycle entry points; the bug was the install path missing the rule the update path already enforced. Sharing one helper removes the asymmetry rather than copying a second guard. The update flow drives install via `installTrackedSkillFromClawHub` with the tracked owner, so requested and tracked owners match and updates still proceed. Bare-slug installs (no `ownerHandle`) keep their prior force-overwrite behavior, matching the update path which also skips the owner check when no owner is requested.

## Verification
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/skills/lifecycle/clawhub.test.ts`: 52 passed with the fix; the new test fails on pristine main.
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs src/skills/lifecycle/clawhub.ts src/skills/lifecycle/clawhub.test.ts`: clean.
- `oxfmt --check src/skills/lifecycle/clawhub.ts src/skills/lifecycle/clawhub.test.ts`: clean.
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json` and `-p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json`: clean.

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: owner-qualified `install --force` over a skill tracked under a different owner must refuse instead of silently downloading over it and rewriting its owner tracking.
Real environment tested: the real `skills install` CLI command action (`registerSkillsCli` -> `installSkillFromClawHub`) run via tsx with no mocks, against the live ClawHub registry, on pristine main@3f0e740f83 and on PR head 75ba27e99b, with a real on-disk workspace (`OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`) seeded with an `@alice/weather` install (real origin.json + lock.json at version 1.0.0 and Alice's own SKILL.md).
Exact steps or command run after this patch: `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=<workspace> node --import tsx <cli> skills install @bob/weather --force --global`, with `<workspace>` pre-seeded with an `@alice/weather` install.
Evidence after fix:
```
# BEFORE (pristine main 3f0e740f83)
$ openclaw skills install @bob/weather --force --global
Downloading weather@1.0.0 from ClawHub...
Installing to <workspace>/skills/weather...
Installed weather@1.0.0 -> <workspace>/skills/weather
exit code: 0
# lock.json after: weather.ownerHandle = "bob"   (was "alice")
# origin.json after: ownerHandle = "bob", installedAt rewritten
# skills/weather/SKILL.md after: Alice's content replaced by the downloaded skill

# AFTER (this patch, identical inputs, head 75ba27e99b)
$ openclaw skills install @bob/weather --force --global
Skill "weather" is tracked as @alice/weather, not @bob/weather.
exit code: 1
# no "Downloading"/"Installing" lines: refused before any network or backup-replace
# lock.json after: weather.ownerHandle = "alice", version 1.0.0, installedAt 123 (untouched)
# origin.json after: ownerHandle = "alice", installedVersion 1.0.0 (untouched)
# skills/weather/SKILL.md after: Alice's content intact
```
Observed result after fix: on main the live force install reports success and clobbers Alice (download runs, lock/origin owner flips to bob, Alice's SKILL.md is overwritten); with the patch the CLI exits 1 with the tracked-owner error before any download, and Alice's lock, origin, and SKILL.md are all left untouched. The colocated regression test in `src/skills/lifecycle/clawhub.test.ts` also asserts this and fails on pristine main, with 52/52 passing on the patched tree.
What was not tested: a same-owner force reinstall and a bare-slug force install over a tracked skill (both intentionally still allowed; covered by the colocated unit tests, not re-driven through the live CLI here).
